title: Landscape Contracting Internship II
description: (Prerequisites: LA 1711 , LA 2701 and 2.0 GPA). Internship of planned, progressive and supervised learning with a landscape contracting firm

title: Special Topics in Landscape Architecture:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Landscape Architecture Construction III - Hydrology
description: (Prerequisites:Grade of C or better in the following course: LA 2644 ). Two hours lecture. Four hours studio. Calculations for storm- water management, best management practices, surface and subsurface drainage systems, basic hydrology and erosion and sediment control design and practices

title: Landscape Architecture Design III - Small Town/Rural Context
description: (Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in the following courses: LA 2644 , LA 2652 , and LA 2654 ). Eight hours studio. Emphasis on design at the Community/Town scale, including place theory and aesthetics

title: Design of the Golf Environment
description: (Prerequisite: LA 1803 ). Three hours lecture. Defining site development concerns of a golf complex, addressing areas of history, design, construction and maintenance

title: Urban Planning Theory
description: Three hours lecture. Open to non-majors. Survey of principles and practice of urban planning. Emphasis on the planning process and use of a city's police power to regulate use of land

title: Case Studies of Executed Works in Landscape Architecture
description: (Prerequisite:Grade of C or better in the following courses:  LA 3655). Special five to ten day on-site observation visit for the study of notable LA projects and construction methods with lectures

title: Planting Design Fundamentals in Landscape Architecture
description: (Prerequisites:Grade of C or better in the following courses: LA 1533 , LA 2554 ,and PSS 2423 ) One hour lecture,four hours studio. Using plants as landscape architectural functional elements in a holistic design context.Applying the design elements and principles to design with emphasis on planting design

title: Landscape Architecture Design IV: Urban Design
description: (Prerequisites:Grade of C or better in the following course: LA 3554 ). Eight hours studio/lab. Emphasis on urban planning and design, including consideration of urban fabric, building typologies, transit, streetscapes, pedestrian circulation, open space, hydrology, and natural systems

title: Landscape Contracting Seminar II
description: (Prerequisite: LA 2712). One hour lecture. Weekly seminar to investigate topics related to modern landscape practices experienced in LA 2712 LC Internship II. Formal presentations of internship case studies

title: Landscape Contracting Internship III
description: (Prerequisites: LA 2711 , LA 3701 and 2.0 GPA). Internship of planned, progressive and supervised experiential learning with a landscape contracting firm

title: Landscape Contracting I
description: (Prerequisites: ABE 1073 and EG 1513). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Study of the nature, scope, and application of the varied construction materials used in landscape projects; and, the construction processes related to landscape development

title: Landscape Contracting Field Trip I
description: (Prerequisite: LA 1701 ). Five to ten day trip to visit landscape contracting firms and observe completed works

title: Landscape Architecture Internship
description: (Prerequisite: Satisfactory completion of semester six of B.L.A. program with an overall G.P.A. of 3.0 in the Junior Year). Supervised experimental learning with a professional office or public agency

title: Directed Individual Study in Landscape Architecture:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Design Theory and Criticism.
description: (Prerequisite:  Undergraduates:  Consent of Instructor, Graduates:  None). Three hours lecture.  An examination of the major theories and criticisms of modern landscape design with emphasis upon developing a critical approach to the profession

title: Landscape Architecture Construction V:  Construction Documents
description: (Prerequisites: LA 2544 and LA 2644 ).  Two hours lecture.  Four hours studio.  The course integrates design principles with construction practices, culminating in the preparation of a site design and set of construction documents

title: Landscape Architecture Construction IV
description: (Prerequisites:Grade of C or better in the following course: LA 3534 or consent of instructor). Two hours lecture. Four hours laboratory. Preparation of landscape architectural construction plans, details, and specifications for outdoor lighting, for irrigation, and for septic systems

title: Exterior Design-Build Studio
description: (Prerequisite:Consent of instructor).Six hours studio/lab. An interdisciplinary exploration of an exterior project focused on sustainable site practices from design concept to implementation

title: Community Food Systems
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Exploration of aspects in community food systems including planning and design, sustainable growing practices, and human nutrition and health. (Same as FNH 4463 /6463 and PSS 4463 /6463)

title: Ecological Planting Design
description: (Prerequisites:  Undergraduates: ART 1123 , LA 1153 , LA 1333 , LA 1533 , PSS 2423 ; Graduates: LA 8513 or Consent of Instructor).  Two hours lecture.  Four hours studio.  Examine and apply adaptation theory and strategies that emphasize resilient approaches to urban and suburban green infrastructure projects

title: Applications for GIS for Landscape Architects
description: (Prerequisite: LA 1223 or consent of instructor). One hour lecture,four hour studio/lab. Applying geographical information systems technology to the practice of Landscape Architecture

title: Study Abroad: Gardens and Urban Spaces
description: (Prerequisite:  Junior or graduate standing of consent of instructor).  Special on-site travel study to experience and document notable landscape architecture projects, methods of construction, and professional offices overseas

title: Landscape Contracting Seminar III
description: (Prerequisite: LA 3712). One hour lecture. Weekly seminar to investigate topics related to modern landscape practices experienced in LA 3712 LC Internship III. Formal presentations of internship case studies

title: Landscape Contracting Field Trip II
description: (Prerequisite: LA 3721 ). Five to ten day trip to visit with landscape contracting firms and observe completed works

title: Professional Practice of Landscape Architecture
description: Three hours lecture. Office management, contracting, budgeting, design proposals, supervision of construction contracts, professional liability,  and professional ethics

title: Landscape Contracting II
description: (Prerequisites: LA 3713 or LA 4334). Two hours lecture. Four hours laboratory. Analysis of legal, financial, and management aspects of landscape contracts; and quantity surveying, cost estimation, and critical path management of landscape construction projects

title: Landscape Contracting III
description: (Prerequisites: LA 4724 , ACC 2013 , and MGT 3113 ). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Theory and practice of managing a Landscape Construction Firm. Case studies of contemporary issues

title: Landscape Contracting IV
description: (Prerequisite: LA 4724 and PSS 4414). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Application levels studies of post-construction management practices of landscape projects

title: Sustainable Landscape Management
description: (Prerequisite:LA 2433). Online course. An examination of methods for sustainable land management. Ecological systems, services, and processes providing the foundation for decision-making in land management

title: Design V-Regional
description: (Prerequisite:Grade of C or better in the following course LA 3654 ). Eight hours studio. Application of spatial analytical techniques, Geographic Information Systems (GIS), and Low Impact Design (LID) strategies architecture at the regional scale

title: Sustainable Communities
description: Three hours lecture. Theory and practices that minimize resource use and pollutant production in the human landscape (same as ABE 4843 /6843)

title: Landscape Architecture Capstone Studio
description: (Prerequisite:Grade of C or better in the following courses: LA 3544, LA 4723 , and LA 4754 ). Eight hours studio. Emphasis on development of an approved terminal project used to demonstrate competency in proposal development, design process, site planning, detail design and construction detailing

title: Special Topics in Landscape Architecture:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: First Year Seminar
description: One hour lecture. First-year seminars explore a diverse arrary of topics that provide students with an opportunity to learn about a specific discipline from skilled faculty members

title: The Gardening Experience
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Basic home garden design and practice toward growing your own food as well as creating simple outdoor plant aesthetics, planting and maintenance

title: Plant Science
description: Two hours lectures. Two hours laboratory. Scientific principles as the basis for practice in producing, handling, processing, marketing, and utilizing agronomic and horticultural plants

title: Turf Management Lab
description: Two hours laboratory.  (Pre or co-requisites PSS 2113 ).  This lab gives the student hands on experience with grass and weed identification and turfgrass management operations.  Turfgrass calculations and equipment calibration will be mastered

title: Introduction to Turfgrass Science
description: Three hours lecture.  Introduction to basic principles associated with the art and science of turfgrass management including propagation, establishment, renovation, and basic pest management

title: Horticultural Impacts on Society
description: Three hours lecture. An in-depth inquiry into the various areas of sociohorticulture and the impact people-plant interactions have on us and our society environmentally, socially, physically, and economically

title: Floral Design
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours studio. The history and appreciation of floral art through exploration of design principles, plant materials, and compositional floral forms

title: Plant Materials I
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Characteristics, identification, and landscape uses of ornamental trees, shrubs, vines, flowers, and grasses adapted to Southern conditions

title: Precision Agriculture I
description: (Prerequisite:  Sophomore standing and MA 1313 ).  Two hours lecture.  Two hours lab.  This introductory course highlights site-specific crop management techniques.  Topics include:  Best Management Practices, economic and physical farm production models, and measurement of variability (same as ABE 2543 )

title: Special Topics in Plant and Soil Sciences:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Fruit Science
description: Three hours lecture. Principles and practices involved in the production of deciduous trees and small fruits

title: Introduction to Weed Science
description: (Prerequisites: BIO 2113 ; CH 1213 or CH 1053 ). Three hours lecture. Managing weeds; basic weed biology; methods of controlling weeds, introductory herbicide technology, weed control systems, and the fate of herbicides in the environment

title: Soils Laboratory
description: (Prerequisite: Prior credit for/or current enrollment in PSS 3303 , Co-requisite for Plant and Soil Sciences majors: PSS 3303 ). Two hours laboratory. General treatment of selected phases of the subject matter

title: Soils
description: (Prerequisite: One semester (preferably two) of inorganic chemistry, CH 1043 , Co-requisite for Plant and Soil Sciences majors: PSS 3301 .) Three hours lecture. General treatment of all phases of the subject including lime and fertilizers

title: Interior Planting Design and Maintenance
description: Two hours lecture, two hours laboratory. Identification of plant materials for interior planting and principles of design, installation and maintenance, preparation of cost estimates and maintenance contracts for interior plantings

title: Wedding Floral Design
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 2343 ). One hour lecture. Four hours laboratory. Application of design principles to wedding floral design

title: Turf Seminar I
description: One hour lecture. Class discussions with invited turf industry representatives. Topics will include Turf industry overview, turf career opportunities, writing a resume, and job interviews. May be repeated for credit more than once

title: Floristry Internship
description: (Pre-requisites: PSS 2343 and consent of Retail Floristry Management faculty).  Individual work experience in a floral industry enterprise with an approved employer under faculty supervision

title: Turn Seminar II
description: One hour lecture. Review of turfgrass literature and presentations of scientific articles. May be repeated for credit more than once

title: Agronomy Internship
description: (Prerequisites: Junior standing and consent of Agronomy Faculty). Individual work experience in an agronomic or environmental organization with an approved employer under faculty supervision. This course may be repeated under approved conditions

title: Horticulture Internship
description: (Prerequisite: Consent of the Horticulture faculty). Individual work experience in a horticulture or allied industry organization with an approved employer under faculty supervision. This course may be repeated under approved conditions

title: Permanent Botanical Floral Design
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 2343 ). One hour lecture. Four hours laboratory. Application of design theory and principles to non-perishable, dried, and preserved floral products

title: Plant Materials II
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 2423 ). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Continuation of PSS 2423

title: Seminar
description: (Prerequisite: Nine credits in horticulture). One hour lecture. Review of horticultural literature, and presentation and discussion of scientific articles

title: Wines and Vines
description: (Prerequisite: Must be age 21 or older by first class meeting). Three hours lecture. Principles and standard practices of wine grape production, processing, and sensory evaluation for students with a professional interest in wine

title: Sustainable and Organic Horticulture
description: Three hours lecture. Online course. A study of the base knowledge of the principles and practices of sustainable, organic, and alternative horticulture management systems

title: Plant Propagation
description: (Prerequisite: BIO 2113 ). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Basic principles in the propagation of horticultural plants

title: Directed Individual Study in Plant and Soil Sciences:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Principles of Floral Design II
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 2343 ). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. An in-depth inquiry into advanced floral design theory, advanced floral design techniques, and requirements for becoming a certified floral designer through the American Institute of Floral Design

title: Floral Management
description: Three hours lecture.  Online course.  To identify and understand the basic principles necessary to operate wholesale and retail floral businesses

title: International Horticulture
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 1313 ). Three hours lecture. Online course. Worldwide overview of horticultural export, marketing, and international trade issues and individual country analyses of specific fruit, vegetable and ornamental crops

title: Sympathy Floral Design
description: (Prerequisite PSS 2343 ). Two hours lecture.  Two hours laboratory.  Application of design theory and principles used in sympathy work

title: Floral Design for Special Events
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 2343 ).  Two hours lecture.  Two hours laboratory.  Planning and preparing of floral design compositions for use in special events

title: Post-harvest Care of Cut Floral Crops
description: Two hours lecture.  Two hours laboratory.  Identification, postharvest care and handling, sourcing and distribution of cut floriculture plant materials

title: Forage and Pasture Crops
description: (Prerequisite: Junior standing or permission of instructor). Two hours lecture . Two hours laboratory. Origin, uses, and ecology of forage plants, establishment, nutritive value, use, yield and maintenance of forage plants as related to morphology, physiology and pasture management

title: Agricultural Crop Physiology
description: Three hours lecture. Online course. Physiology of agricultural plants, including water relations, respiration, photosynthesis and growth and development

title: Grain Crops
description: (Prerequisite: Junior standing). Two hours lecture . Two hours laboratory. Corn, small grain, practice in commercial grading given in laboratory

title: Fiber and Oilseed Crops
description: (Prerequisite: Junior standing). Three hours lecture. Production and utilization of fiber and oilseed crops. Emphasis on soybean, cotton, and peanut production

title: Advanced Fruit Science
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 3043 or equivalent). Three hours lecture. Three hours laboratory. A study of the latest advances in pomology and interpretation of current research findings and their application to modern fruit growing

title: Sustainable Agroecology
description: (Prerequisites: PSS 1313 and PSS 3303 ). Online. Three hours lecture. The study of interactions between crops and abiotic and biotic environments. Emphasis is placed on quantitatively examining theory and principles for production, stability and sustainability of agricultural ecosystems

title: Seed Production
description: (Prerequisite: Junior standing). Two hours lecture . Two hours laboratory. Principles and practices, special emphasis on production of varietally pure seeds; agronomic factors in harvesting, drying, storage, treating and marketing seed

title: Soil Fertility and Fertilizers
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 3303 and junior standing). Three hours lecture. Fundamentals and concepts of soil fertility/nutrient management; fertilizer sources and crop response to nutrients; fertilizer production; and soil fertility evaluation and maintenance through organic and inorganic fertilization

title: Microbiology and Ecology of Soil
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 3303 ). The study of diverse soil microbial communities and how they influence the structure and function of ecosystems (natural and managed) and the global biosphere (same as BIO  4324/6324)

title: Soil Classification
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 3303 ). Three hours lecture. Origin, development, and classification of soils including identification and field mapping

title: Soil Conservation and Land Use
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 3303 ). Two hours lecture . Three hours laboratory. Soil identification, topographic relationships and soil-water resources; their characteristics, quality, suitability, and management; conservation practices; using soil maps to determine land use

title: Controlled Environment Agriculture Laboratory
description: (Prerequisite: Prior credit for/or current enrollment in PSS 4343 ; Corequisite for horticulture majors: PSS 4343 ). Two hours laboratory. Online course. An experiential study of the principles and practices of controlled environments operation and management

title: Controlled Environment Agriculture
description: (Prerequisites: BIO 2113 and PSS 3303 ; Co-requisite for horticulture majors: PSS 4341 ). Three hours lecture. Online Course. A detailed review and explanation of principles and practices of controlled environments operation and management

title: Arboriculture and Landscape Maintenance
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Care of ornamental trees and shrubs, including pruning, bracing, surgery, transplanting, and fertilization

title: Sustainable Nursery Production
description: Prerequisites: PSS 2423 and PSS 3303 ). Three hours lecture. Online course. Nursery crop production including site selection and planning, plant nutrition, water relations and irrigation, shipping, and managing people and resources

title: Geospatial Agronomic Management
description: (Prerequisites PSS 3303 , PSS 3133 ). Three hours lecture. This class will utilize the basic tools of geographical information systems and geographical positioning systems technologies to analyze agronomic case studies

title: Agriculture Remote Sensing I
description: (Prerequisites: MA 1313 and any GIS course or consent of instructor). Two hours lecture. Two hours lab. Online course. The study of whole-farm systems using data captured by camera, spectroscopic satellites, and telemetric sensors. Emphasis is placed on integrating multiple ag-related geospatial concepts into field-level production inquiries

title: Agriculture Remote Sensing II
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 4383 /6383 or GR 4313 /6313). Two hours lecture and two hours laboratory. The advanced study of whole-farm systems using data captured by camera, spectroscopic satellites, and telemetric sensors. Emphasis is placed on integrating multiple ag-related geospatial concepts into field-level production inquiries

title: Remote Sensing Seminar
description: One hour lecture. (Prerequisite: Junior Standing). Lectures by remote sensing experts from industry, academia, and governmental agencies on next-generation systems, applications, and economic and societal impact of remote (Same as ECE 4411 /6411, FO 4411 /6411, GR 4411 /6411)

title: Turfgrass Management
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 2113 ).  Three hours lecture.  An advanced comprehensive study of turfgrasses and the varied management strategies employed for golf and sports turf, home lawns, commercial turf, and sod production

title: Golf Course Operations
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 4413 /6413). Two hours lecture . Two hours laboratory. Scheduling maintenance practices, golf course construction and renovation with emphasis on operation and care of specialized turf equipment

title: Athletic Field Management
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 2113 or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. A comprehensive study of athletic fields, including construction, maintenance, renovation and management. Emphasis will be placed on interactions between soil properties and sports turf performance

title: Vegetable Production
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 3303 and PSS 3301 or PSS 4113 /6113 or BIO 4214 /6214). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Principles and practices of commercial vegetable production

title: Community Food Systems
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Exploration of aspects in community food systems including planning and design, sustainable growing practices, and human nutrition and health. (Same as LA 4463 /6463 and FNH 4463 /6463)

title: Hydroponic and Soilless Crop Production
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 4343 /6343). Three hours lecture. The fundamentals of growing crops using hydroponics and soilless culture, particularly in controlled environments such as greenhouses and vertical farms

title: Introduction to Remote Sensing Technologies
description: (Prerequisite: Senior or graduate standing, or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. Electromagnetic interactions, passive sensors, multispectral and hyperspectral optical sensors, active sensors, imaging radar, SAR, Lidar, digital image processing, natural resource applications (Same as ECE 4423 /6423 and ABE 4483 /6483)

title: Plant Genetics
description: (Prerequisites: PSS 1313 , BIO 1134 or BIO 2113 ). Three hours lecture. An introductory application of genetics and genetic principles as they apply to plant reproduction and crop production. Understanding of mitosis and meiosis, diploid and polyploid genetics, and the generation of transgenic crops

title: Plant Breeding
description: (Prerequisite: PO 3103 ). Three hours lecture. Application of genetic principles to the improvement of economic crop plants; history, methods and procedures of plant breeding

title: Precision Agriculture II
description: (Prerequisite:  PSS/ ABE 2543 and Junior Standing).  Two hours lecture.  Two hours lab.  Site-specific management techniques are examined.  Continuous decision-making processes of farm production are integrated using a whole-system, geospatial approach (same as ABE 4543 /6543)

title: Plant Growth and Development
description: Three hours lecture. Online course. Structure of plant developmental processes and how environmental factors interact to affect and control plant growth and development

title: Soil Chemistry
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 3303 ).  Three hours lecture. Soil is a mixture of inorganic and organic solids, air, and water. Soil may contain micro and macro organisms and root. All of these things interact and influence each other

title: Floriculture Crop Programming
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 4343 /6343). Two hours lecture. Three hours laboratory. A detailed study of the techniques involved in the production of the major commercial flower crops

title: Weed Biology and Ecology
description: (Prerequisites: BIO 2113 . PSS 3133 . Junior standing or consent of instructor). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory . Weed identification and population responses to agricultural production systems

title: Ag. Flight Technologies I
description: (Prerequisites: Junior Level or higher). Two hours online lecture, two hours online laboratory. The course instructs students in Small Unmanned Aerial Systems (sUAS) manual flight skills and the FAA Remote Pilot certification exam materials, and is a prerequisite course for advanced autonomous flight training ( PSS 4743 /6743)

title: Ag. Flight Technologies II
description: (Prerequisites: PSS 4733 /6733 Ag. Flight Tech. I AND Instructor Approval). Three hours lecture. Enrolled students attend a week-long advanced autonomous flight workshop at Mississippi State University, Starkville campus. At week’s end, students take the FAA Part 107 Remote Pilot Certification. (Course may be taken two times)

title: Undergraduate Research in Plants & Soil Sciences
description: Hours, credits and deliverables to be arranged. The purpose of this course is to provide a student with the opportunity to participate in research and/or creative project beyond the traditional undergraduate experience, while allowing the university to track undergraduate participation in these activities

title: Herbicide Technology
description: (Prerequisites: PSS 3133 and junior standing). Two hours lecture. Three hours laboratory. Classification and use of herbicides. A detailed look at herbicide application-field use and factors influencing herbicide activity. Credit may not be given for this course and PSS 4823 /6823

title: Turfgrass Weed Management
description: (Prerequisite: PSS 3133 and Junior standing). Two hours lecture. Three hours laboratory. Classification and use of herbicides with emphasis on herbicides and emphasis on herbicides used in turfgrasses.  Credit may not be given for this course and PSS 4813 /6813

title: Special Topics in Plant and Soil Sciences:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Animal Agriculture & Society:  Food for Thought
description: Three hours lecture.  Exploration of connections between animal agriculture and society including animal production systems, food and fiber production, consumer perceptions and addressing concerns regarding the growing world population.  (Same as ADS 1013 )

title: Special Topics in Poultry Science:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Seminar
description: One hour lecture. Preparation and presentation of specially assigned current problems in poultry science

title: Seminar
description: One hour seminar. Preparation and presentation of specially assigned current problems in poultry science

title: Genetics I
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. (Prerequisites: MA 1313 or higher, BIO 1134 or higher or BIO 2113 or higher ). Principles of heredity, genetic material, and gene expressions. (Same as BIO 3103 , GNS 3103 )

title: Commercial Poultry Production
description: Three hours lecture. An introduction to practical management problems encountered in the production of commercial eggs, broiler production, and breeding flocks

title: Poultry Production Internship
description: (Prerequisite: Consent of instructor). Structured, progressive experiential learning with the live production division of a poultry integrator

title: Poultry Processing Internship
description: (Prerequisite: Consent of instructor). Structured, progressive experiential learning with the processing of a poultry integrator

title: Poultry Evaluation I
description: Two Hour Lecture.  Two Hour Laboratory.  Grading/evaluation of live poultry and market products using different methods and grading scales based on USDA standards and the American Standard of Perfection

title: Poultry Evaluation II.
description: Two Hour Lecture.  Two Hour Laboratory.  (Prerequisites: PO 3423 or consent of instructor).  Grading/evaluation of live poultry including turkeys and market products using different methods and grading scales based on USDA standards and cull factors

title: Directed Individual Study in Poultry Science:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Seminar
description: One hour seminar. Preparation and presentation of specially assigned current problems in poultry science

title: Diseases of Poultry
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Survey of cause, effects, diagnosis, prevention, and control of common poultry diseases

title: Seminar
description: One hour seminar. Preparation and presentation of specially assigned current problems in poultry science

title: Management of Commercial Layers
description: Three hours lecture. Management of laying flocks as related to production of edible eggs; including housing, cage design, equipment, feeding techniques, lighting, molting and other factors involved with efficient production

title: Avian Reproduction
description: Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Principles of avian reproductive physiology and applications in poultry management to maximize reproductive performance. Reproductive characteristics of several bird species are included

title: Broiler Production
description: Three hours lecture.  Two hours laboratory.  Practical management problems encountered in the production of broilers including breeding, housing, brooding, diseases, and feeding; field trips to intensified broiler areas

title: Poultry Nutrition
description: Three hours lecture. Study of the digestion, absorption, and metabolism of nutrients in avian species. Special emphasis is given to practical nutritional needs of commercial poultry flocks

title: Feed Manufacturing
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Mill design and equipment; procurement, storage and quality control for ingredients and complete feeds; formulation of practical type poultry rations

title: Feed Mill Quality Assurance
description: Three hours lecture. The principles of feed and ingredient quality assurance and how to develop a comprehensive quality assurance program will be discussed. Other topics will include ingredient characteristics and quality specifications, feed manufacturing quality assurance procedures, and tools used within a quality assurance program

title: Feed Mill Leadership and Management
description: Three hours lecture. The principles of operating and managing a modern feed mill will be discussed. This course will also include other topics: compliance with government regulations, team building, time management, leadership styles, preparing capital requests and feed mill budgets, and developing key performance indicators for commercial and integrated feed mills

title: Poultry Products Safety and Sanitation
description: (Prerequisite:  Junior standing or greater.)  Two hours lecture.  Poultry product safety hazards, food safety systems (HACCP), principles and practices of food sanitation related to poultry products and poultry safety regulations (same as FNH 4512 /6512)

title: Poultry Processing
description: Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Study of commercial poultry processing including poultry inspection, regulations, processed poultry products, egg processing, and food safety. (Same as FNH 4514 /6514)

title: Avian Anatomy and Physiology
description: Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Anatomy and physiology of the fowl with emphasis on morphology, structure, and function of the avian body. (Same as PHY 6844 )

title: Special Topics in Poultry Science:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Introduction to Information and Decision Science in Agroecosystems
description: Three hours lecture. Introductory course to the science of helping people learn how to access, analyze, apply and amend information to solve problems in agriculture

title: First Year Seminar
description: One hour lecture. First-year seminars explore a diverse array of topics that provide students with an opportunity to learn about a specific discipline from skilled faculty members

title: International Agricultural Systems
description: Introduction to world agriculture, farming systems and technologies, crops, trade, and food production and processing. Influence of population and climate on global agriculture. Ethical issues surrounding environmental, social, political, and financial aspects of agriculture. (Same as GA 2103 )

title: Applications of Computer Tech to Agricultural Education, Leadership, and Communications
description: Three hours lecture. Application of micro- computer technology in agricultural and extension education; data storage and retrieval; and use of canned computer programs in agricultural and educational settings

title: Orientation to Agricultural Education, Leadership, and Communications
description: One hour lecture. An introduction to key personnel in the School of Human Sciences, College of Agriculture and Life Sciences, and across the University. An overview of services provided by departments in the School, College, and University. In addition, various career opportunities in agricultural education, leadership and communications

title: Foundations of Agricultural Education
description: Two hours lecture. Foundations of school-based agricultural education (SBAE) teaching; an overview of preparing teachers for a changing world including knowledge of learners, subject matter, and teaching within the context of agricultural science

title: Personal Leadership and Communications
description: Two hours lecture. An introduction to personal leadership and communications. Human Sciences, College of Agriculture and Life Sciences, and across the University. Students will develop their capacity for leadership and communication including but not limited to personality types, strengths, values, engaging in crucial conversations, and perspective taking

title: Orientation to Agricultural Education, Leadership & Communications
description: Three hours lecture.  History and principles of agricultural education programs; program development, management, and community involvement; career opportunities in agricultural education, leadership and communications

title: Special Topics in Agricultural Education, Leadership, and Communication:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged.  This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses.  (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Field Experience in Agricultural Education, Leadership and Communications
description: (Prerequisite:  Consent of Instructor).  Supervised field experience for agricultural education, leadership and communications students in approved settings; pre-internship experiential learning opportunity.  (May be repeated one time)

title: Professional Writing in Agriculture, Natural Resources, and Human Sciences
description: (Prerequisites:  Completion of EN 1103 and 1113 or equivalent and Junior Standing).  Three hours lecture.  Basic principles and techniques in communicating information relevant to agriculture/agribusiness, natural resources, and life sciences

title: Professional Presentations in Agriculture and Life Sciences
description: (Prerequisite: AELC 3203 ). Three hours lecture.  Strategies and techniques for effective presentations in agriculture, life sciences and natural resources. Emphasis on oral and visual techniques for formal and non-formal situations

title: Internship in Agricultural Leadership and Industry
description: (Prerequisite: Junior Standing or with consent of instructor) Three hours Dir. Exp Study, Pract., Co-Op. Capstone course providing students with a supervised learning experience solidifying and applying concepts learned throughout their coursework in a professional atmosphere

title: Graphic Design in Agriculture and Life Sciences
description: Three hours lecture. An introduction to graphic design software and techniques specific to industries in agriculture and life sciences. Students will use industry-standard software to prepare text and graphics and package them for use in print production and also for use in fabric and textile manufacturing

title: Internship in Agricultural Leadership and Organizations
description: (Prerequisite: Junior Standing or with consent of instructor) 3 Hours Dir. Exp Study, Pract., Co-Op. Capstone course providing students with a supervised learning experience solidifying and applying concepts learned throughout their coursework in a professional atmosphere

title: Internship-Agricultural Communications
description: (Prerequisites:  junior standing).  Supervised field experiences related to participation in professional activities relating to problems, methods, and basic communications skills in agriculture and life sciences.  Course is not repeatable

title: Foundations of Leadership in Agricultural and Life Sciences
description: Three hours lecture.  Foundational theories and principles of leadership emphasizing personal characteristics, leadership styles, power and influence, group dynamics, and managing change for effective leadership in agriculture

title: Team Leadership for Agriculture & Life Sciences
description: Three hours lecture. Strategies and techniques for building and leading a successful team. Self-assessment, team-building skills, and experiential activities in teamwork are emphasized and contextualized specifically in agriculture and life sciences

title: Directed Individual Study in Agricultural Education, Leadership, and Communications:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credit to be arranged

title: Principles and Practices of Extension Education
description: (Prerequisite: Junior standing).  Three hours lecture. Developing, implementing, and evaluating Extension and non-formal educational programs for youth and adult audiences. Comprehension and application of experiential and transformative learning models and teaching methods

title: Applications of Computer Tech to Agricultural Education, Leadership, and Communications
description: Two hours lecture and two hours laboratory. Application of microcomputer technology in agricultural education, leadership, and communications; data storage and retrieval; and use of canned computer programs in agricultural and educational settings

title: Communications Strategies in Agriculture and Life Sciences
description: (Prerequisites: Senior standing and consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. Evaluation and development of communications strategies for agriculture and life science organizations, issues, and/or products. Integrating communications techniques and teamwork are emphasized

title: Development of Youth Programs
description: Three hours lecture. Needs and interests of youth; developing, managing, and evaluating formal and non-formal youth education programs; volunteer and paraprofessional staff development; securing and developing supportive services

title: Teaching Methods in Agricultural and Human Sciences
description: (Prerequisite: College of Ag and Life Science major and junior standing). Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Planning instruction; selection teaching techniques; developing teaching plans; teaching agricultural/human science topics; using instructional technologies; and evaluating learner progress. (Same as FDM/ HDFS 4424 )

title: International Agricultural Education
description: Three hours lecture. Examination of formal and non-formal agricultural education and related processes that influence global agricultural development including impacts of culture and changing demographics. Analysis of current global agricultural issues, roles of international organizations, and effectiveness of technology transfer

title: Teaching Agricultural Mechanics
description: Two hours lecture and three hours laboratory. Methods and strategies for teaching middle and high school students skills in agricultural mechanics

title: Study Tour:  1-3 hours.
description: Experiential learning through travel in the United States or abroad focusing on specialized areas of study in Agricultural Education

title: Pedagogy of AgriScience Programs
description: (Prerequisite: Senior standing or permission of instructor). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Theory and practice in planning objectives and materials, and teaching methods for organizing, delivering, and managing agricultural science programs. Restricted to AELC/AEE teaching concentration students

title: Contemporary Issues in Agriculture and Life Sciences
description: Three hours lecture. Course designed to immerse students in investigation of the issues the agriculture industry faces. Students will uncover the historical aspect of current policy and legislation, identify pertinent contemporary issues, and analyze and describe problems, impacts and solutions

title: Professional Seminar in Agricultural Education
description: (Prerequisites:  Admission to Teacher Education and senior standing).  Three hours lecture.  Legal, professional, administrative and curricular issues in agricultural and extension education.  Includes philosophy, classroom management, curriculum planning, community involvement and problem solving to plan formal and informal education programs

title: Teaching Internship in Agricultural Education
description: Supervised observation and directed teaching in respective field of endorsement.  (Prerequisites:  Admission to Teacher Education and senior standing; Co-requisite: AELC 4896 )

title: Teaching Internship in Agricultural Education
description: Supervised observation and directed teaching in respective field of endorsement.  (Prerequisites:  Admission to Teacher Education and senior standing; Co-requisite: AELC 4886 )

title: Special Topics in Agricultural Education, Leadership, and Communication:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged.  This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses.  (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: First Year Seminar
description: One hour lecture. First-year seminars explore a diverse array of topics that provide students with an opportunity to learn about a specific discipline from skilled faculty members

title: Visual Design in Dress
description: Three hours lecture. Application of basic art principles to selection and design of clothing; physical, cultural, social, aesthetic, and psychological aspects of dress

title: Apparel Construction
description: One hour lecture. Four hours laboratory. Principles of clothing construction; problems involving fabric selection, use of industrial patterns, and basic fitting

title: Product Development I
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Introduction to the product development lifecycle in relation to the apparel industry. Emphasis is placed on technology applications at various stages of product development

title: Fashion Apparel Analysis
description: Three hours lecture. Terms and processes relevant to the apparel product development life-cycle and quality indicators/control measures for finished apparel products

title: Intro to Buying and Management
description: (Prerequisites: FDM 2553 ). Three hours lecture. Concepts and theories in apparel buying and management; roles and responsibilities of merchandise buyers; domestic and foreign merchandise resources and negotiation

title: Textiles for Apparel
description: Three hours lecture. Two hours lab. An introductory study of textile fibers, yarns, fabrics, colorants, and finishes; and the factors that influence the selection, appearance, care, and serviceability of textiles for apparel

title: Introduction to Merchandising
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of the entire consumer goods industry as it relates to merchandising

title: Fashion Portfolio Development
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Creation of printed and electronic portfolios and related materials for fashion-related careers. Includes project selection; layout and graphics; photography; photo-editing; writing; use of appropriate software

title: Product Development II
description: (Prerequisites: FDM 2123 ). Three hours lecture. Analysis of product development and manufacturing related to the apparel industry including terminology, design processes, product development, sewn product analysis and quality control

title: Fashion Forecasting
description: (Prerequisite: FDM 2553 ). Three hours lecture. Survey of the techniques and procedures for identifying and forecasting fashion trends and evaluating current trends in lifestyles, business, ready-to-wear, and art. Students will learn to recognize and analyze predictive fashion trends that affect retail merchandising and marketing decisions

title: Internship Preparation
description: (Prerequisite: consent of instructor). One-hour lecture. Preparation for an internship in a chosen specialization

title: Merchandise Retail Pricing and Inventory Management
description: (Prerequisites: FDM 2553 and ST 2113 or MA 2113 or BQA 2113 or consent of instructor). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Specific problems, procedures and practices in merchandise pricing and inventory management

title: Visual Merchandising
description: (Prerequisite: FDM 2553 ). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Principles of window and interior display, individual and group participation in designing and executing displays for commercial and educational purposes

title: Historic Costume
description: Three hours lecture. Survey of costume from prehistoric to modern times with emphasis on social, cultural, political, and technological changes impacting fashion, preservation, documentation, and exhibition of artifacts

title: Excel and Retail Operations
description: Three hours lecture. Extensive study of Microsoft Excel as it relates to Retail Operations with an emphasis on the practical retail-specific business applications. The course will provide comprehensive coverage of features available within the application as they relate to the retail operations such as formulas/functions, and data interpretation/presentation

title: Merchandising and Promotion Strategies
description: (Prerequisite: FDM 2553 and junior standing or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. A study of fashion presentation techniques and production requirements in the primary, secondary and retail settings

title: Directed Individual Study in FDM
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Pattern Making and Design
description: (Prerequisite: Grade of B of higher in FDM 1533 ). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Advanced problems and techniques for clothing construction and creative expression through application of drafting and flat pattern design techniques

title: Draping
description: (Prerequisite: FDM 4343 ). One hour lecture. Four hours laboratory. Principles of apparel design through the three-dimensional manipulation of fabric on industry standard dress forms. Analysis of fit and design, problem solving and interaction of fabric characteristics with style features

title: Teaching Methods in Agricultural and Human Sciences
description: (Prerequisite: CALS major and junior standing). Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Planning instruction; selecting teaching techniques; developing teaching plans; teaching agricultural/human sciences topics; using instructional technologies; and evaluating learner progress. Same as AELC 4424

title: Fashion Consumer Behavior
description: (Prerequisites: SO 1003 or PSY 1013 and junior standing). Three hours lecture.  Application of concepts and theories from the social sciences related to the study of consumer behavior related to dress, textile and apparel products, and retail experiences. Experience in conducting consumer research

title: Merchandise Planning and Buying
description: Three hours lecture. Capstone course in planning, buying and managing inventory in a fashion retail environment

title: Fashion Entrepreneurship
description: Three hours lecture. Application of principles of entrepreneurship with emphasis on retail/fashion; exploration of issues in entrepreneurship relative to apparel, retailing, and design; development of skills necessary to establish and maintain successful business

title: Creative Design Techniques
description: (Prerequisite: FDM 1533 or consent.)  Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Students will learn a variety of surface and other design techniques and apply them to existing and original garments and accessories. Students will also utilize historical and other inspirations for their designs

title: Global Sourcing in the Textile and Apparel Industry
description: (Prerequisites: FDM 2553 and FDM 4513 or equivalent or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. Evaluation of global issues facing the textile complex-fiber, textile and apparel industries. Students will learn about international trade and global issues in the textile complex

title: Digital Merchandising
description: Three hours lecture. A study of electronic merchandising and its application to consumer products and services for business to business and business to consumer. Introduction to electronic merchandising theory, terminology, resources, industry participants and career opportunities

title: FDM Senior Showcase
description: (Prerequisite: Graduating senior status). Two hours laboratory. Hands-on laboratory to prepare final senior portfolio presentations for faculty review. Fashion Design and Merchandising majors only

title: Computer-Aided Design for Fashion
description: (Prerequisite: FDM 4343 ).  Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Applications of various computer-aided design software to the fashion industry, including illustration and the design of fabric, garments, and accessories

title: FDM Internship
description: (Prerequisite: FDM 3221 ). Individual work experience in an approved apparel, textiles, or merchandising setting under supervision of Miss. State University faculty. (Course may be taken for credit up to two times)

title: Undergraduate Research in FDM
description: Hours, credits and deliverables to be arranged. The purpose of this course is to provide a student with the opportunity to participate in research and/or creative project beyond the traditional undergraduate experience, while allowing the university to track undergraduate participation in these activities

title: Special Topics in Fashion Design and Merchandising:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: First Year Seminar
description: One hour lecture. First-year seminars explore a diverse array of topics that provide students with an opportunity to learn about a specific discipline from skilled faculty members

title: Individual and Family Development through the Lifespan
description: Three hours lecture. Introduction to individual and family development through the lifespan, conception to death, focusing on social and emotional development, contextual influences on development, and application

title: Trauma Informed Practice
description: Three hours lecture. This course prepares students to recognize signs of trauma and apply trauma-informed practices when working with children and families. The course is designed for students majoring in HDFS, psychology, and other disciplines where knowledge of the impact of trauma on children and families is pertinent

title: Perspectives on Child Maltreatment and Child Advocacy
description: Three hours lecture. This course is the introductory course for child advocacy studies. This course covers the history, comparative perspectives, legal framework, reporting, identification and effects of child maltreatment and professional/community responses to child maltreatment

title: Prenatal and Infant Development
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Biological and environmental influences; behavioral and developmental patterns, from the onset of pregnancy to toddlerhood

title: Child Development
description: (Prerequisite: HDFS 1813 or consent of instructor). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Developmental characteristics of children with emphasis on the early years; implications for care and guidance

title: Special Topics in Human Development and Family Science:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Field Experience:  1-6 hours.
description: (Prerequisite:  Consent of Instructor).  Supervised field experience for Human Development and Family Science students in approved settings; pre-internship learning experience

title: Global Child Advocacy Issues
description: Three hours lecture. Exploration of global child advocacy issues and the multidisciplinary cultural approaches used to address them

title: Consumer Economics
description: Three hours lecture.  Economic principles as they apply to consumer situations, and the consumer's relation to the American and world economy

description: Prerequisite: HDFS 2813 . Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Selection of appropriate equipment, materials, and activities; program planning for birth to age 5; observation and participation at the Child Development and Family Studies Center

description: (Prerequisites: HDFS 2813 or HDFS 1813 ). Three hours lecture. An intensified exploration of human development theory, research and methodology used in the study of individuals across the lifespan

title: Methods & Materials for Early Care and Education Programs
description: (Prerequisites: HDFS 2813 , HDFS 3803 and junior standing.) Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Designing curriculum and programming for children birth to 5 years of age with emphasis on children’s developmental characteristics as related to appropriate learning experiences

title: Human Development in the Context of Leisure and Recreation
description: (Prerequisite: HDFS 1813 ). Three hours lecture. Introduces historical, theoretical, and empirical content focused on leisure and recreation as a context for human development across the lifespan

title: Guiding Young Children’s Behavior & Social Development
description: (Prerequisites: HDFS 2803 and HDFS 2813 ). Three hours lecture. Examine and design appropriate guidance techniques based on developmental growth patterns and individual differences in young children from birth to 5 years old

title: Language and Literacy in the Early Years
description: Three hours lecture. Children’s development of language and literacy through the early years, including roles of parents, community, childcare providers, and teachers

title: Directed Individual Study in Human Development and Family Science:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Child Advocacy & Science of Trauma (CAST) Capstone
description: (Pre-requisite: Completion of required courses in CAST minor). 1 hour lecture and 2 hours laboratory. Provides students an opportunity to apply trauma-informed knowledge in a practice setting. Examines current research, theoretical perspectives, and a bio-psycho-social approach to trauma-informed practice

title: Child Maltreatment Prevention and Intervention
description: (Prerequisite: Junior standing or higher.) Three hours lecture. Professional responses to child maltreatment through intervention and prevention programming, focusing on caregiver education, awareness campaigns, and community education

title: Family Resource Management
description: Three hours lecture. Decision-making in the family and operation of the household as affected by family values, philosophies, resources, and socio-economic conditions

description: (Prerequisite: Junior/senior writing or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. An examination of the impact of legislation and public policy on the well being of the family with emphasis on policy and family change

description: Three hours lecture. An introduction to the dynamics of the aging process and strategies for maximizing life satisfaction during aging

title: Teaching Methods in Agricultural and Human Sciences
description: (Prerequisite: College of Ag and Life Science major and junior standing). Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Planning instruction; selection teaching techniques; developing teaching plans; teaching agricultural/human science topics; using instructional technologies; and evaluating learner progress (Same as AELC/FDM/ HDFS 4424 /6424)

description: (Prerequisites: Senior standing and admission to teacher education). Two hours lecture. Basis for curriculum planning; exemplar curriculums; and customizing curriculums

title: Family Life Education Methodology
description: (Prerequisite: HDFS major and junior standing). Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Principles and practices of effective family life education programming. Identifying needs of diverse populations, designing educational programs and materials, teaching human development and family science content, facilitating group processes, and evaluating learner and program outcomes

title: Internship Preparation
description: (Prerequisite: Junior standing and consent of instructor). One hour lecture. Preparation for an internship in a chosen specialization

title: PreK-K Teacher Candidacy Internship
description: (Prerequisite: Senior standing and consent of instructor). Individual work experience in an approved preschool/PreK-K setting under supervision of Mississippi State University faculty

title: Child Development Internship
description: (Prerequisite:  Successful completion of all academic coursework and consent of instructor.)  Individual work experience in an approved child development setting under supervision of Mississippi State University faculty

title: Child Life Internship
description: (Prerequisite: Successful completion of all academic coursework and consent of instructor). Individual work experience in an approved child life setting under supervision of Mississippi State University faculty

title: Youth Development Internship
description: (Prerequisite: Successful completion of all academic coursework and consent of instructor.)  Individual work experience in an approved youth development setting under supervision of Mississippi State University faculty

title: Family Science Internship
description: (Prerequisite: Successful completion of all academic coursework and consent of instructor.) Individual work experience in an approved family services setting under supervision of Mississippi State University faculty

title: Grief and Bereavement:  Support Systems and Practices for Children and Families
description: Two hours lecture. An introduction to current issues, theory, and research related to grief, loss, and bereavement as it impacts children, adolescents, and their families

title: Parenting
description: (Prerequisite: HDFS 1813 and junior/senior writing, or consent of instructor).  Three hours lecture.  Study of the child as a part of the family in a dynamic human ecological system

title: Adult Development:  The Middle Years
description: Three hours lecture. Theory and perspectives on adulthood in contemporary society, adjustment to internal and environmental changes, role structures, supportive networks and public policy issues

title: Development and Administration of Child Service Programs
description: (Prerequisite: HDFS 3813 or concurrent enrollment). Three hours lecture. Planning, administering, and evaluating the organizational structure of a variety of child service programs

title: Child Life Foundations
description: (Prerequisites: HDFS 2813 , junior standing and permission of instructor). One hour lecture. Foundations and history of child life practice including impact of illness on child and family, elements of therapeutic play and medical preparation

title: Child Life Clinical
description: (Prerequisites: HDFS 2813 , HDFS 4833 , junior standing and permission of the instructor). Four hours laboratory. This course provides the student with a child life practicum experience in a pediatric health care facility

title: The Hospitalized Child
description: (Prerequisites: HDFS 3803 and HDFS 3813 or concurrent enrollment, junior standing or permission of the instructor). Three hours lecture. A pre-practicum development approach to the special needs of the hospitalized infant, child and adolescent

title: Family Interaction
description: Three hours lecture. Interaction within functional families; focus on the family as a system, on family varieties and roles, and on effective interactions

title: The Family:  A Human Ecological Perspective
description: Three hours lecture. The impact of internal and external factors on the development of individual and family relationships throughout the life cycle

title: Consumer Aspects of Aging
description: Three hours lecture. Analysis of the decisions, issues and research related to the consumer aspects of aging from a global and national perspective

title: Positive Youth Development
description: (Prerequisite: HDFS 1813 and junior/senior writing class or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. Examines theoretical and empirical foundations of the growing field of Positive Youth Development; examines school and community-based programs that foster PYD

title: Risk, Resilience and Preventive Interventions
description: (Prerequisite: HDFS 1813 and junior/senior writing; or consent of instructor).  Three hours lecture. Theory and research relevant to understanding risk and resilience in human development and family studies application of risk/resilience framework to individual and family preventive interventions

title: Teaching Internship in FCS Education
description: (Prerequisites: Admissions to Teacher Education, minimum grade point average of 2.5 overall and in major, and completion of all professional education courses with a grade of C or better). Supervised observation and directed teaching in respective field of endorsement

title: Adolescent Development
description: (Prerequisite: Junior/Senior/Graduate student status; or consent of instructor). Theory and research relevant to understanding the developmental processes and changes of adolescence from a contextual and systemic perspective

title: Teaching Internship in FCS Education
description: (Prerequisites: Admission to Teacher Education, minimum grade point average of 2.5 overall and in major, and completion of all professional education courses with a grade of C or better). Supervised observation and directed teaching in respective field of endorsement

title: Special Topics in Human Development and Family Science:  1-9 hours.
