title: Survey of Human Sciences
description: One hour lecture. Introduction to the field of Human Sciences through a study of its history and the variety of professional careers available

title: Professional Protocol
description: One hour lecture. The essentials of professional protocol and accepted standards of social usage

title: Science of Food Preparation
description: (Prerequisites:  Grade of “C” or better in CH 1213 /1221 or HS major).  One hour lecture.  Four hours laboratory.  A study of foods and the principles underlying handling and preparation of food product to maintain the highest standards of quality.  (Same as FNH 2203 )

title: Child Health and Nutrition
description: Three hours lecture. Nutrition requirements during pregnancy and lactation, and of infants and young children; birth defects from metabolic errors; related health of young children. (Same as FNH 2283 )

title: Individual and Family Nutrition
description: Three hours lecture. Fundamental principles of human nutrition and the practical application of this knowledge in the selection of adequate diets. (Same as FNH 2293 )

title: Interior Design Fundamentals
description: Three hours lecture. Introduce a practical approach to the application of interior design in the built environment. (For non interior design students). (Same as ID 2603 )

title: Textiles for Interiors
description: (Prerequisite CH 1043 ).Three hours lecture. Two hour laboratory. Study of fibers, yarns, fabric structures,dyes, and finishes related to the textile industry. Emphasis on testing and evaluation of interior textiles

title: Environments for Special Needs
description: Three hours lecture. Laws, attitudes, conditions, specifications, and environmental issues affecting private and public spaces. (Same ID 3673 )

title: Consumer Issues and Policy
description: (Prerequisite:HS 3303 or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. An assessment of policies and programs relating to information, product safety, and channels of appeal for the individual

title: Work Experience in Human Sciences Related Occupations:  3-6 hours.
description: (3-6) Work experience in two phases of occupational human sciences, development of a program of work, and incorporating the work experience into curricula

title: Current Housing Problems of Families
description: (Prerequisite: Junior standing). Three hours lecture. Analysis of current housing problems confronting families, their historical development, government policies and remedial measures

title: Research and Application in Human Sciences
description: (Prerequisite: Senior standing in FDM or HDFS). Two hours lecture. Focus on conducting original research to integrate fundamental Human Sciences principles. Emphasis on professional development and effectiveness in Human Sciences professions. (Same as FDM 4702 and HDFS 4702)

title: Study Tour:  1-3 hours.
description: Experiential learning through travel in the United States or abroad focusing on specialized areas of study in human sciences

title: Internship:  5-8 hours.
description: (Prerequisite: Minimum of senior standing in the major and consent of instructor). Individual work experience in an approved setting under supervision of Mississippi State University faculty

title: First Year Seminar
description: One hour lecture. First-year seminars explore a diverse array of topics that provide students with an opportunity to learn about a specific discipline from skilled faculty members

title: Architecture Appreciation
description: Three hours lecture. Illustrated study of architecture's role in shaping the quality of man's environment. Architectural history, design theory, and process as it affects daily life. Intended for non-majors. (Same as ARC 1013 )

title: Building Construction Studio A
description: (Prerequisites: MA 1323 or ACT Math sub-score 24). Twelve hours laboratory. Introduction to construction materials and methods, construction drawing and modeling, building systems, project life cycles and management, and professional thinking and action

title: Building Construction Studio B
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 1116 ). Twelve hours laboratory. Development of building assemblies and construction sequencing, drawings and computer applications, project management skills, and professional thinking and action

title: Construction Drawings
description: (Prerequisites: MA 1323 or ACT Math sub-score 26).  Three hours lecture. Reading technical drawings, 2D/3D visualization, and manual drafting techniques

title: Introduction to the Built Environment
description: Three hours lecture. Introduction to the construction industry, including methods, practices, trends, careers, and key stakeholders in the design and construction process, with orientation to essential elements of professional practice

title: Construction Site Safety
description: (Prerequisites: BCS-1133). Three hours lecture. This course will present and discuss fundamental topics related to construction safety including construction site safety plans

title: Construction and Culture
description: Three hours lecture. A study of the development construction throughout history. The purpose of this course is to broaden the students’ awareness of the global significance of construction

title: Building Construction Studio 1
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 1126 ). Twelve hours laboratory. In depth examination of building construction materials and methods, systems, construction drawing and details, and construction finishes

title: Construction Materials and Methods
description: Three hours lecture.  Introduction to Construction Materials and Methods, focusing on the properties, applications, and sustainability of materials commonly used in the construction industry

title: Construction Quality and Codes
description: (Prerequisites: BCS-2123 Construction Materials and Methods) Three hours lecture. This course presents knowledge on controlling of construction projects to ensure compliance with building codes, drawings and specifications

title: Building Construction Studio 2
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 2116 ). Three hours of lecture and six hours of laboratory. In-depth study of estimating, cost control, financial management, and equipment management in construction projects

title: Construction Science Technology
description: (Pre-requisite: BCS-2123) Three hours lecture. Use of a collection of innovative tools and software to improve the construction process throughout the project’s lifespan

title: Virtual Design and Construction
description: Prerequisites: BCS 1116 ). Three hours lecture. Use of digital tools to create virtual models of buildings for improving the construction process throughout the lifespan of the project

title: Environmental Building Systems I
description: (Prerequisite:  Architecture majors- ARC 1546 and PH 1123 ; BCS majors- PH 1123 ; others-instructor consent).  Three hours lecture.  Investigation of the morphological impacts of various environmental energies on building forms/systems.  Includes light, climatic, and ecological factors.  Same as ARC 2713

title: Construction Internship/Co-Op
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 1126 and consent of instructor). Supervised professional work experience of 480 hours with a professional construction firm in which the student obtains on-the-job experience in aspects of construction management. This course can substitute BCS 2116 or BCS 3126

title: Building Construction Studio 3
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 2226 ). Three hours of lecture and six hours of laboratory. In-depth study of health and safety, project management, construction management, plant and equipment management, logistics and operations management, and building pathology

title: Building Construction Studio 4
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 3116 ). Twelve hours laboratory. In-depth evaluation of the principles and applications of construction productivity, estimating and bidding procedures, cost alternatives, scheduling, sequencing, budgeting and project cashflow management

title: Construction Estimating
description: (Prerequisite: BCS-2123) Three hours lecture. Introduction to quantity take-offs, pricing, estimating, and cost management in the construction discipline

title: Construction Statics
description: (Prerequisite: MA-1613 with a C or higher) Three hours lecture. The evaluation of structures, the properties of materials and the structural behavior of load-resisting members as it relates to buildings

title: Mechanical and Electrical Systems
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 2713 ). Three hours lecture. A detailed examination of the design and construction of building electrical systems

title: Construction Finance
description: (Pre-requisite:ACC-2023) Three-hour lecture. An in-depth exploration of construction financial management, introducing methods for managing a company's financial resources and developing its accounting system

title: Construction Scheduling
description: (Prerequisite: BCS-3133 Construction Estimating) Three hours lecture. In-depth study of project time management, construction management, plant and equipment management, logistics and operations management, and scheduling tools and methods

title: High Performance Construction
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 3116 ).  Three hours lecture.  Advanced building fabrication and construction systems are explored including high-performance construction materials such as fiber-reinforced cement, fiber-reinforced plastics, polymeric materials, geosynthetics, masonry materials and coatings

title: Environmental Building Systems II
description: (Prerequisites:  ARC majors- ARC 2546 and ARC 2713 ; BCS majors- BCS 2713 and BCS 2116 ; others-consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. Concentrates on defining the mechanical and electrical (active) techniques available to architects for integrating thermal comfort and life safety into the built form.  (Same as ARC 3723 )

title: Structures I
description: (Prerequisite: MA 1613 or MA 1713 and either ARC 1546 or BCS 1126 ). Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Application of the principles of statics and the strength of materials on structural elements. Construction material. (Same as ARC 3904 )

title: Structures II
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 3904 or BCS 3904 ). Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Design and analysis of structural elements as part of frames and other structural systems. (Same as ARC 3914 )

title: Directed Individual Study in Building Construction Science:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Building Construction Studio 5
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 3126 ). Three hours of lecture and six hours of laboratory. In-depth evaluation of the legal and contractual environment for construction activities/projects. Emphasis on specifications; dispute resolution; construction contracts and procurement systems; and project delivery modeling

title: Building Construction Studio 6
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 4116 ). Three hours of lecture and six hours of laboratory. In-depth study of project controls, risk management, strategic management, construction accounting, facilities and maintenance management, and international construction and contracting

title: Construction Contracts
description: (Prerequisite:BCS-2123 Construction Materials) Three hours lecture. In-depth study of construction contracts and its components, construction management, and various project management knowledge areas

title: Professional Communication and Practice
description: (Prerequisite: BCS 3126 ). Two hour lecture. Construction practice is reviewed in the broader context of ethics and the built environment. Emphasis on developing professional communication

title: Professional Practice
description: (Prerequisistes: BCS 3126 ) Three hours lecture. Construction ethics are reviewed in the broader context of architecture relative to social responsibility. Additional exploration includes professional ethics and emerging best practices

title: Construction Project Management
description: (Prerequisite: BCS-3233 Cons Scheduling) Three hours lecture. In-depth study of project management and its components, construction management, and various project management knowledge areas

title: Special Topics in Building Construction Science:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: First Year Seminar
description: One hour lecture. First-year seminars explore a diverse arrary of topics that provide students with an opportunity to learn about a specific discipline from skilled faculty members

title: Art History Pre-1300's
description: Three hours lecture. The study of art from prehistoric times to the Renaissance through the architecture, sculpture, painting and minor arts of the western world

title: Art History Post 1300's
description: Three hours lecture. Art from the Renaissance to the present studied chronologically through the architecture, painting, sculpture, and minor arts of the western world

title: Art Appreciation
description: Three hours lecture. An illustrated lecture course dealing with periods, styles, and personalities in painting, sculpture, and architecture. Honors section available

title: Design I
description: Six hours studio. A basic study of the fundamental elements and principles of design with an emphasis on compostion

title: Design II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1123 ). Six hours studio. A continued study of the fundamental elements and principles of design with an emphasis on the theory and application of color

title: Three-Dimensional Design
description: (Prerequisites: ART 1123 or ARC 2536 ). Six hours studio. An study of the organization of the principles and elements of art as they apply to three-dimensional artwork

title: Drawing I
description: Six hours studio. A freehand drawing course for all students interested in the visual arts. This course offers the basic vocabulary for a graphic notation

title: Drawing II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1213 ). Six hours studio. A continuation of ART 1213 further developing conceptual and perceptual use of drawing tools, processes and materials. Black and white, and color media explored

title: Painting Survey
description: (Prerequisites: ART 1123 and ART 1213 ). Six hours studio. The fundamentals of oil paintings and composition

title: Global Contemporary Art
description: Three hours lecture. This course examines the role of visual culture in the age of globalization. Throughout the semester, students will learn various approaches to visual culture studies using theoretical, practical, creative, and philosophical frameworks

title: Photography Survey
description: (Prerequisites: ART 1123 ). Two hour lecture. Two hour studio. The fundamentals and aesthetics of photography relating to graphic design and the fine arts

title: Introduction to the Business of Museums
description: This introductory course is a general overview of museums as businesses and the professional positions involved in museum operations today. (This course counts as an Art History for Art Majors)

title: Life Drawing I
description: (Prerequisites: ART 1213 and ART 1223 ). Six hours studio. A drawing class with emphasis on the basic forms and proportions of the human figure

title: Drawing III
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1223 ). Six hours studio. A continuation of ART 1223 to develop further drawing skills and concepts for advanced students

title: Printmaking Survey
description: (Prerequisites: ART 1123 , ART 1133 , and ART 1223 ). Six hours studio. Introduction to the basic techniques and concepts of intaglio and relief printmaking

title: Sculpture Survey
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1123 or ART 1153 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Introduction to the basic concepts, materials, and processes of sculpture by exploring modeling, casting, carving and constructing

title: Appreciation of Arts & Crafts Movement
description: Three hours lecture. The study of the growth and development of Arts & Crafts through the ages with instructional applications, and practical designs. Non-art majors only

title: Ceramic Art Survey
description: Six hours studio. Introduction to the processes of ceramic art including hand built forms, wheel thrown pottery and glazing

title: Animation
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1153 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Introduction to the basic methods and practices of movement with traditional and digital techniques from hand-drawn to stop-motion. Demonstrate creating frame-by-frame sequences in various forms

title: Digital Design I
description: Six hour studio. Introduction to digital creative applications in the visual arts and design

title: Digital Design II
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2803 or consent of Instructor). Six hour studio. Further instruction in digital creative applications and beginning concept development in design thinking for graphic design majors

title: Introduction to Film
description: Three hour lecture. Two hours lab. A basic introduction to the study of film language, history, and theory, emphasizing aesthetic, technological, and socio-cultural developments of film form. (Same as CO 2904 and EN 2904 )

title: Special Topics in Art:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Painting II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2013 or consent of the instructor). Six hours studio. Observational based painting. A study of mediums and techniques in painting in continuation of ART 2013

title: Non-Representational Painting
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2013 or consent of the instructor). Six  hours studio. Introduction to non-representational painting. Intermediate painting with further emphasis on the skills and techniques of painting

title: Figurative Painting
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2013 and ART 2213 or consent of the instructor). Six hours studio. Introduction into painting the figure. A continuation of ART 2013 to further develop skill in use of the medium and formal organization of subject matter in painting

title: Watercolor Painting
description: (Prerequisites: ART 1123 and ART 1213 or consent of the instructor). Six hours studio. The technique and use of various water-soluble painting mediums

title: Digital Painting
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2013 or consent of the intructor). Six hours studio. An intermediate course exploring the use of the computer and peripheral devices in the creation of raster and vector base drawing and painting

title: Italian Renaissance Art History
description: Three hours lecture. The history of art in Italy in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, emphasizing the religious monuments of the period. (Same as REL 2143)

title: History of Graphic Design
description: (Prerequisite:Pass second year portfolio review, open only to Graphic Design Majors or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. A survey of the history of graphic design from pre-writing to digital

title: Life Drawing II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2213 or consent of the instructor). Six hours studio. Further study in rendering the human figure

title: Darkroom Basics
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2103 or consent of instructor.) Six hours studio. The course is an introduction to the traditional photographic darkroom

title: Studio Lighting
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2103 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. The course is an introduction to the professional studio lighting techniques

title: Intermediate Darkroom
description: (Prerequisites: Art 2103 and ART 3223 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Advanced techniques of photographic processes in black and white with emphasis on aesthetics

title: Photogram
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2103 or consent of the instructor). Six hours studio. The course explores the photogram technique as a photographic tool to create a fine art image

title: Scanography
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2103 or consent of the instructor). Six hours studio. The course explores the scanner as a photographic tool to create a fine art image. Students will learn a variety of approaches to creating fine art photographic images using a scanner

title: Street Photography
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2103 or consent of the instructor) Six hours studio. This class will explore Street Photography from a fine arts perspective. Students will photographically explore issues and events that are significant to our present culture and society. Images created will follow a documentary approach

title: Printmaking II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2303 ). Six hours studio. Continued exploration of the print as a medium of creative expression

title: Graphic Design I
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2803 and ART 2813 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Introduction to the art and process of design in printed communication. Students develop graphic skills with an emphasis placed upon conceptual development, research, production and both visual and verbal presentation skills

title: Graphic Design II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 3313 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. The execution of a series of design projects promoting an awareness of different forms of printed visual communication

title: Printmaking III
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2303 ) Six hours studio. Exploration of traditional and contemporary relief printmaking techniques, with emphasis on woodcut and collograph methods. Aesthetic, technical, and conceptual development stressed

title: Illustration
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2013 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. A course introducing issues and instrumentation related to standards in the professional field of illustration emphasizing mixed-media processes

title: Ceramic Art II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2503 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Elementary glaze formulation, surface decoration, kiln firing, wheel thrown and hand built form

title: Sculpture II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2403 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Further exploration of concepts and processes of sculpture, including mold making and armature building. Beginning development of personal language of expression

title: 3D Seminar
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2403 or ART 2503 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Research and investigation of the origins, community, process, and message of contemporary three-dimensional art and craft

title: Directed Writings in Modern Art History
description: Three hours lecture. History of the 20th-21st Century Art with emphasis on scholarly writing, reading, and analyzing of contemporary models and varieties of writing

title: Art and Film
description: Three hours lecture. This course explores the rich and complex relationship between the visual arts and film

title: Art in France: 1850-1900
description: Three hours lecture. This class explores one of the most  dynamic periods of artistic production in the entire history of art

title: History of Photography
description: Three hours lecture. The history of still photography as a fine art from its beginning to present

title: Art of the Graphic Novel
description: Three hours lecture.  An introduction to the history of the extended comic strip, or graphic novel, and to examples of this art form's major works

title: Roman Baroque Art
description: Three hours lecture. An examination of the art and architecture created in Rome in the 17th Century

title: Medieval Stained Glass
description: Three hours lecture.An examination of the history of Medieval stained glass from the points of view of technique, material,style, and cultural/historical significance

title: The Gothic Cathedral
description: Three hours lecture. An examination of the art, architecture, religion, politics, and culture of 13th century French Gothic Cathedrals

title: The History of Art and Religion
description: Three hours lecture. An examination of the histories, functions, and controversies of visual imagery created in the service of religion/spirituality

title: Art Field Study
description: (Prerequisite:  Successful completion of Art Concentration Portfolio Review).  One hour lecture.  A field study of the masters in fine art, graphic design and photography fields in the context of a significant, national art center

title: Gallery Management
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1123 and ART 1213 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. The study of gallery operations, techniques of curation, artists ethics, installation procedures and gallery management of an art gallery

title: Study Abroad Seminar I
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1213 or consent of instructor.) Three hours seminar. CAAD study abroad program. Course content will vary to reflect the expertise of the instructor (Same as ARC 3813 and ID 3813 .)

title: Study Abroad Seminar II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1213 or consent of instructor.) Three hours seminar. CAAD study abroad program. Course content will vary to reflect the expertise of the instructor. (Same as ARC 3823 and ID 3823 )

title: Digital Photography
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2103 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. The techniques and aesthetics of digital imagery emphasizing the use of digital photographic input and output processes

title: Introduction to Print Production
description: (Prerequisites: ART 3323 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. This class is an introduction to digital print techniques, client work, and the responsibilities and role of graphic designers

title: Directed Individual Study in Art:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Performance Art and Criticism
description: Three hours lecture. This course will examine performance art and criticism from the early 20th century to the present. Students will study various approaches to performance art using critical analysis, theoretical inquiry, group discussions, and local field trips

title: History of Digital Art
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1013 , ART 1023 , ART 2063 , or consent of instructor.) Three hours lecture. A survey of digital art practices from the 1950s to the present. Students will learn theoretical, practical, and philosophical influences on digital art

title: Watermedia Painting
description: Six hours studio. An in-depth exploration of water based painting media utilizing watercolor and acrylic mediums in the creation of a body of two dimensional artworks

title: Visualizing Resistance in the Global South
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1023 , ART 2063 or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. This course critically examines the art of resistance, protest, and revolution in contemporary visual culture produced in the Global South. Students will investigate responses to political division, sexism, racism, nationalism, and religious discrimination via artistic intervention

title: Senior Research
description: (Prerequisites: Senior Standing and consent of instructor, corequisite ART 4620 ). Three hours lecture. The application of research methods for the fine artist in contemporary society

title: Senior Thesis
description: (Prerequisites: Senior Standing  and consent of instructor, corequisite ART 4620 ). Three  hours lecture. Execution of a thesis exhibition and portfolio materials

title: Typography I
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2803 , ART 2813 ).  Six hours laboratory. The art and process of presenting written communication in graphic form

title: Typography II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 4103 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Advanced problems in presenting written communication in graphic form

title: Screen Printing for Graphic Design
description: (Prerequisite: ART 3313 , ART 4103 , or consent of Instructor). Six hours studio. An in-depth look at contemporary methods in designing and screen printing posters and shirts

title: Topics in Film
description: Three hours lecture. Two hours lab. Repeatable under different subtitles with advisor approval. An advanced investigation of specific topics in Film, Film History, Directors, Genre, and/or approaches to its production. Readings and discussions, supplemented by lectures/labs and film screenings. (Same as CO 4124 /6124 and EN 4124 /6124)

title: Letterpress for Design
description: (Prerequisite: ART 3313 , ART 4103 , or consent of Instructor). Six hours studio. An in-depth look at contemporary methods of designing and letterpress printing identity, postcards and posters

title: Art in the City
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1013 , ART 1023 , ART 2063 , or consent of instructor.) Three hours lecture. Juniors and seniors only. This course will examine the development and implementation of urban arts projects within a diverse array of social, culture, and political contexts

title: Visual Storytelling
description: (Prerequisites: ART 3313 , ART 4103 , or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Exploration of methods, processes and fundamental skills integral to visual storytelling as it relates to advertising and branding. Students will gain experience creating conceptually driven branded stories, including writing, producing, art directing, and editing

title: Interactive Design I
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2813 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. The design of UI/UX with an emphasis on graphic and interactive media to build user interface prototypes from websites to apps

title: Alternative Photography
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2103 or consent of instructor.) Six hours studio. Alternative photographic processes in black and white with emphasis on aesthetics

title: Advanced Printmaking
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2303 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Exploration of advanced printmaking concepts and techniques. Emphasizes refining a personal aesthetic using previously learned print processes with etching and engraving techniques

title: Drawing IV
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2233 at both levels and consent of instructor for 6343). Six hours laboratory. A continuation of ART 2233 to develop further skills for advanced students

title: Illustration for Design
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2813 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. This course will address the concepts, principles, and techniques of illustration for design, emphasizing the visual language to make a harmony between graphic elements and illustration

title: Motion Graphics
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2813 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Introduction to the concepts and techniques of motion design with the elements of shapes, forms, and type, emphasizing the visual storytelling by dynamic movements

title: Advertising Design I
description: (Prerequisite: ART 3323 and ART 4103 /6103, and consent of instructor). Six hours laboratory. Course requiring ideational, image making, graphic design and typographic skills to meet rigorous conceptual/visual standards pertinent to creating a brand of a company's identity

title: Advertising Design II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 4403 /6403 and consent of instructor). Six hours laboratory. An advanced course requiring interaction on a professional level, working with realistic agency-client situations in order to develop efficient, distinguishable and competitive promotional campaigns

title: Presentation Skills for Designers
description: (Prerequisites: ART 3313 , ART 4103 , or consent of instructor) Three hours lecture. Development of pitching and presentation skills, achieved through practice, coaching, and critique. Students will learn to pitch their ideas, their campaigns, their portfolios, and themselves

title: Alternative Color
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2103 or consent of instructor). One hours lecture. Six hours studio. Alternative photographic processes in color with emphasis on aesthetics

title: Ceramics-Handbuilding
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2503 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Advanced skills and professional practices focused on non-wheel forming techniques for creative expression in clay. May be taken twice for credit

title: Ceramics-Wheel Technique
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2503 or consent of instructor.) Six hours studio. Advanced skills and professional practices focused on the potter’s wheel as a tool for creative expression. May be taken twice for credit

title: Ceramics-Glaze Formation
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2503 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Advanced skills and professional practices focused on the chemistry of ceramic glazes and developing various application techniques. May be taken twice for credit

title: Ceramics-Professional Practices
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2503 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Advanced skills with a focus on the development of a marketable aesthetic and professional practices. May be taken twice for credit

title: Graphic Design Internship
description: (Prerequisite: ART 3323 or consent of instructor). Supervised instruction Graphic Design. May be taken once for three hours GD concentration elective credit. May be taken a second time for three hours of Art Studio Elective credit

title: Ceramic Art III
description: (Prerequisites: ART 3503 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Advanced problems in glaze formulation, kiln technology and wheel thrown and hand built forms

title: Critical Issues in Recent Art
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1013 , ART 1023 , ART 2063 , or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. Discussion of major developments and issues in contemporary art, focusing on the period 1980 to present

title: Photographic Portfolio I
description: (Prerequisites: senior standing, Photography concentration majors or permission of instructor). Six hours studio. This course is an introduction to the professional practices in photography and the development of a portfolio. May be taken twice for credit

title: Photographic Portfolio II
description: (Prerequisites: ART 4583 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. This course is an extension of the professional practices in photography and the completion with an exhibition of a portfolio from ART 4583

title: Advanced Studio-Drawing:  3-9 hours.
description: (Prerequisite: ART 4343 and consent of instructor). May be taken for credit more than once. Hours and credit to be arranged and shall not exceed a total of nine hours for all advanced studies in any one semester. Advanced study in drawing. Further development of studio skills

title: Advanced Studio - Painting:  3-9 hours.
description: May be taken for credit more than once. Hours and credit to be arranged and shall not exceed a total of nine hours for all advanced studios in any one semester. (Prerequisite: Consent of instructor). This course develops advanced studio skills and professional practice. Course encourages analysis and criticism of aesthetic, social, ethical and related issues

title: Advanced Studio - Fine Arts:  3-9 hours.
description: (Repeatable). (Prerequisite: Senior Standing and consent of instructor).  Hours and credit to be arranged and shall not exceed a total of nine hours for all advanced studies in one semester. Course develops advanced studio skills and professional practice. Taken with Senior Research and Thesis to create capstone artwork

title: Advanced Studio - Sculpture:  3-9 hours.
description: May be taken for credit more than once. Hours and credit to be arranged and shall not exceed a total of nine hours for all advanced studios in any one semester. Six hours studio. Further development of a personal sculptural aesthetic through media of choice

title: Advanced Studio - Graphic Design:  3-9 hours.
description: (3-9). May be taken for credit more than once. Hours and credit to be arranged and shall not exceed a total of nine hours for all advanced studios in any one semester. (Prerequisite: ART 4403 and consent of instructor). This course develops advanced studio skills and professional practice

title: Advanced Studio - Ceramics:  3-9 hours.
description: May be taken for credit more than once. Hours and credit to be arranged and shall not exceed a total of nine hours for all advanced studios in any one semester.  This course develops advanced studio skills and professional  practice. Course encourages analysis and criticism of aesthetic, social, ethical and related issues

title: Advanced Studio - Photography
description: May be taken for credit more than once. Credit and hours to be arranged and shall not exceed a total of nine hours for all advanced studios in any one semester. Six hours studio. This course develops advanced studio skills and professional practice. Course encourages analysis and criticism of aesthetic, social, ethical and related issues

title: Photography Internship
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2103 and consent of instructor). Three hours internship. A supervised professional work experience of 150 hours with a photographer or business in the field of photography. (May be taken for credit more than once)

title: Internship in Fine Art
description: (Prerequisite: Consent of department head). Six hours studio. Supervised instruction and experience for professional art practice. May be taken for credit more than once

title: Advanced Print Production
description: (Prerequisite: ART 3313 or consent of instructor) Six hours studio. A more in-depth look at digital printing techniques through comprehensive work and the responsibilities and roles of graphic design including environmental graphics

title: Advanced Concept Development
description: (Prerequisite: ART 3323 and ART 4103 or consent of instructor) Six hours studio. Conceptual development is the primary goal, additionally students will have more comprehensive campaigns in their portfolios. The techniques can be applied to all design courses

title: Sculpture- Furniture Making
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2403 or ID 4693 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. An in-depth investigation into the design and execution of contemporary studio furniture. May be taken twice for credit

title: Sculpture- Metal Fabrication
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2403 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Introduction to the history and techniques of metalworking including cutting, forming, welding, brazing, finishing, mechanics, kinetics and armature making. May be taken twice for credit

title: Sculpture- Materials and Processes
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2403 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Introduction and exploration of materials and processes used in design and production of contemporary objects. May be taken twice for credit

title: Digital Drawing
description: (Prerequisite: ART 2013 ; ART 2803 ; or ART 2103 ; or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Utilization of computer software, drawing tablets, cameras, and traditional media to create digital imagery

title: Introduction of Multimedia I Design and Authoring
description: (Prerequisite: ART 3313 or Consent of instructor). One hour lecture, five hours laboratory. The design and authoring of interactive multimedia for fine and applied arts using desktop computers. Course encourages analysis and criticism of aesthetic and related issues

title: Advanced Studio - Computer Art and Design
description: (Prerequisite: Consent of instructor). Six hours studio. This course develops advanced studio skills and professional practice. Course encourages analysis and criticism of aesthetic, social, ethical and related issues. Fine Art Majors only. May be taken for credit more than once

title: Digital Imaging
description: (Prerequisite: Art 2103 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. Application of computer software to generate digital imagery captured with lens-based and related media. Additional projects will be required for graduate credit

title: Graphic Design for the Internet
description: (Prerequisite: ART 3313 , open only to Graphic Design Majors, or Consent of Instructor). One hour lecture, five hours laboratory. An introduction to graphic design for the Internet, internet history, HTML, image manipulation, and the use of software to facilitate website design

title: Video Art
description: (Prerequisites: ART 2103 or consent of instructor). Six hours studio. This course will address the use of an approved digital camera, computer, and video editing software for digital video input and editing of video in a fine arts context. Advanced problems will be required of graduate students

title: Graphic Design Capstone
description: (Prerequisite: ART 4403 or and consent of instructor). Six hours studio. This course develops advanced studio skills and professional practice

title: Film Theory
description: Three hours lecture. One hour lab. This course will introduce students to major theoretical positions and modes of analysis used to understand the various frameworks in which to view, criticize, analyze, and (re)contextualize film. (Same as CO 4924 /6924, EN 4924 /6924)

title: Special Topics in Art:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: First Year Seminar
description: One hour lecture. First-year seminars explore a diverse arrary of topics that provide students with an opportunity to learn about a specific discipline from skilled faculty members

title: Interior Design Graphics
description: One hour lecture. Four hours laboratory. Introduction to basic manual drafting and tools/techniques used by interior designers in executing and reading graphic language in two dimensional form

title: Interior Design Studio I
description: (Prerequisite: ID 1683 ). Two hours lecture. Four hours laboratory. Introduction to the basic principles and elements of design using practical and abstract applications in creative problem solving analyzing spatial form and function

title: Design Thinking and Representation
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Introduction to design thinking methodology, concept development, and various forms of graphic representation, including hand sketching for ideation

title: CAD for Interior Design
description: Prerequisite: ID 1683 or consent of instructor. Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Introduction to computer-based design as applied in the interior design field

title: Rendering
description: (Prerequisites: Sophomore Standing) Six hours studio. A studio course dealing with concepts, techniques, and media used in executing interior and exterior renderings

title: Interior Design Fundamentals
description: Three hours lecture. Introduce a practical approach to the application of interior design in the built environment. (For non-interior design majors). (Same as HS 2603 )

title: ID Studio II
description: (Prerequisite ART 1123 and ID 1694 ).  Two hours lecture.  Four hours laboratory.  Introduction to design theory and its application in the development of criteria for interior environments

title: Interior Materials, Treatments, and Resources
description: (Prerequisite: HS 2664 or concurrent enrollment). Three hours lecture. Materials, equipment, services and resources available to the interior designer for meeting clients' needs

title: Textiles for Interiors
description: (Prerequisite CH 1043 or CH 1213 ). Three hours lecture. Two hour laboratory. Study of fibers, yarns, fabric structures, dyes, and finishes related to the textile industry. Emphasis on testing and evaluation of textiles for interior applications

title: Building Codes and Systems
description: (Prerequisites: ID 3363 3/D CAD Modeling & ID 2614 Studio II) Three hours lecture. Systematic integration of building codes and systems on interior design as part of the design process

title: 3/D CAD/Modeling
description: (Prerequisites: ID 2103 or BCS 1116 or consent of instructor). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Advanced computer graphic communication in interior design for the development of technical and perspective drawings created in presentation formats using 3D imaging

title: Digital Design for Interiors
description: Three hours lecture. Introduce innovative software applications for interior design students to create graphic presentations, portfolios, and digital illustrations of interior elements

title: Career and Portfolio Development
description: (Prerequisites: ART 1133 , ART 1123 , ART 1213 , ID 1683 , ID 1694 , and ID 2614 ).  One hour lecture.  Development of an understanding of career possibilities in Interior Design and preparation for the sophomore portfolio review process

title: Interior Design Studio III
description: (Prerequisite: ID 2614 ). Two hours lecture. Four hours laboratory. Integration of the total building environment, through the application of the design elements and technical aspects of the field

title: Interior Design Studio IV
description:
(Prerequisite: ID 3614 ). Two hours lecture. Four hours laboratory. Actual practice of the contract interior design field through the execution of
commercial design problems

title: Interior Design Detailing and Construction Documents
description: (Prerequisites: ID 2103 and ID 3614 ). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Systematic intergration of building systems, construction, technology, and materials on interior systems. Detailing of these systems is an extension of the design process

title: History of Interiors I
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of furniture styles, ornament, designers, and accessories associated with period interiors from the early Egyptian period through 1850

title: History of Interiors II
description: (Prerequisite: ID 3643 or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. Defining advancements/evolutions of design philosophies of furniture and interiors in late 19th adn 20th centuries; addressing presentation skills and techniques for interior design professionals

title: Color and Lighting for Interiors
description: (Prerequisite: ID 2615). One hour lecture. Four hours laboratory. Concentrated study of color and light relationships as they apply to the visual, technical and functional aspects of interior spaces

title: Environments for Special Needs
description: Three hours lecture. Laws, attitudes, conditions, specifications, and environmental issues affecting private and public spaces. (Same as HS 3673 )

title: Study Abroad Seminar I
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1213 or consent of instructor.) Three hours seminar. Six weeks of on-site instruction in Italy as part of the CAAD Italy study abroad program. Course content will vary to reflect the expertise of the instructor (Same as ART 3813 and ID 3813 .)

title: Study Abroad Seminar II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1213 or consent of instructor.) Three hours seminar. Six weeks of on-site instruction in Italy as part of the CAAD Italy study abroad program. Course content will vary to reflect the expertise of the instructor (Same as ART 3823 and ARC 3823 .)

title: Directed Individual Study in Interior Design
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Introduction to Historic Preservation
description: Three hours lecture. An introduction to American historic preservation, its history, principles, and practice. Course may be repeated twice

title: Principles of LEED
description:
One hour lecture. A general study of the principles
of the Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design(LEED) Program including
preparation to take the LEED Green Associate Exam

title: Interior Design Studio V
description: (Prerequisite: ID 3624 ). Two hours lecture. Four hours laboratory. Integration of the total living environment, through the application of the design elements and technical aspects of the interior design field

title: Internship Placement
description: (Prerequisite: Senior Standing in Interior Design and ID 4663). One hour lecture. Professional opportunities as they relate to internships for interior design students. Preparation of resume and portfolio for procurement of internship

title: Interior Design Studio VI
description: (Prerequisite: ID 4644 ). Two hours lecture. Four hours laboratory. Advanced study of the commercial interior design field through individual research and the execution of commercial design problems

title: Senior Portfolio Production
description: (Prerequisite: Senior standing in Interior Design).  One-hour lecture. Development of printed and digital portfolios and related materials necessary in obtaining an internship and career in interior design

title: Professional Practice for Interior Design
description: (Prerequisite: Senior standing in ID). Two hours lecture. Professional opportunities as they relate to individual competencies. Study of studio procedures, ethics, business and legal aspects

title: Integrated Lighting Solutions
description: (Prerequisite: ID 3673 , or ARC 3723 , or IE 3123 or permission of instructor).  Three hours lecture.  Principles of lighting design that addresses lighting analytics and the understanding of lighting as an integrated part of building systems

title: Lessons from the Theatre:  Architectural Lighting Design
description: (Three hours lecture). An introduction to the basics of theatre stage lighting and the theoretical approach of applying these principles to architectural lighting design

title: Furniture Design
description: (Prerequisite: ID 2103 , ID 3643 , ID 3653 , and consent of instructor). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Exploration of the basic methods and processes of furniture design

title: Interior Design Internship
description: (Prerequisites: Senior standing, 2.50 GPA and consent of instructor). Individual work experience in an approved setting under supervision of Mississippii State Univ faculty

title: Historic Lighting Environments
description: Three hours lecture. A split level interdisciplinary course focusing on historic lighting design. The class will study the emerging lighting technologies that increase efficiency, and improve light quality, while respecting the era, textures, architectural scene and landscape of historic structures

title: Special Topics in Interior Design:  1-9 hours.
description: (Credit and title to be arrange. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses.(Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: First Year Seminar
description: One hour lecture. First-year seminars explore a diverse arrary of topics that provide students with an opportunity to learn about a specific discipline from skilled faculty members

title: Concept and Form
description: (Prerequisites: ART 1213 or ART 1123 or ARC 1536 or BSC 2116) Three hours lecture. Introduction and practice for developing and presenting concepts and criticism

title: Architectural Appreciation
description: Three hours lecture. Illustrated study of architecture's role in shaping the quality of man's environment. Architectural history, design theory, and process as it affects daily life. Intended for non-majors. (Same as BCS 1013 )

title: Architectural Design I-A
description: (Prerequisite: Letter of Acceptance into design studio and consent of Director of Architecture). Two hours lecture. Ten hours studio. Introduction to creative process, design principles and methods. Design projects emphasize verbal and visual communication, observing, analyzing, representing, and making of space and architectonically derived form

title: Architectural Design I-B
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 1536 or consent of Director). Two hours lecture. Ten hours studio. Introduction to creative process, design principles and methods. Design projects emphasize verbal and visual communication; observing, analyzing, representing, and making of form, space, materials

title: Representation 1
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Introduction to fundamental applications and design values associated with various representation techniques. Topics will include both analog and digital image manipulation, editing, layout, and publishing tools as part of the professional workflow for architects and designers

title: History of Architecture I
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of the built environment from prehistory through the Early Middle Ages

title: Architectural Design II-A
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 1546 or equivalent or consent of the Director). One hour lecture. Eleven hours studio. Introduction to fundamental aspects of building including structural-spatial ordering systems. Projects emphasize linkages between people and spaces through investigation of perceptual-conceptual issues

title: Architectural Design II-B
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 2713 and ARC 2536 or equivalent, or consent of the director). One hour lecture. Eleven hours studio. Introduction to fundamental aspects of building including structural-spatial ordering systems that build on fundamental design principles taught in previous semesters. Further emphasis is placed on sustainable design methods

title: Environmental Building Systems I
description: (Prerequisite:  Architecture majors- ARC 1546 and PH 1123 ; BCS majors- PH 1123 ; others-instructor consent).  Three hours lecture.  Investigation of the morphological impacts of various environmental energies on building forms/systems.  Includes light, climatic, and ecological factors.  Same as BCS 2713

title: Materials
description: (Prerequisite: Architecture majors - ARC 2536 , Non-architecture majors - ARC 1013 ). Three hours lecture. Introduction to construction materials for Architects. Topics include technical/performance properties, physical characteristics, manufacturing and harvesting, aesthetics, selection and specification, precedents, and environmental impact

title: Special Topics in Architecture:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Representation 2
description:
Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Development of fundamental applications and design values associated with various representation techniques. Topics include digital modeling, building information modeling, and rendering tools as part of the professional
workflow for architects and designers

title: History of Architecture II
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 2313 ). Three hours lecture. Survey of major developments in architecture and city planning from the Fourteenth through the Eighteenth Centuries

title: History of Architecture III
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 3313 ). Three hours lecture. Survey of major developments in American and world architecture and survey of major developments in European architecture beginning from the mid-eighteenth century to the twenty first century

title: Architectural Design III-A
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 2546 or equivalent or consent of the Director). One hour lecture. Eleven hours laboratory. Urban Housing architecture design studio that addresses issues of urbanity, dwelling, spatial and building planning, life safety, regulatory requirements and building codes

title: Architectural Design III-B
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 3713 and ARC 3536 or equivalent, or consent of the director). One hour lecture. Eleven hours laboratory. The development of building design while collaborating with allied professionals on an intermediate scale project by incorporating environmental concerns, building construction assemblies, and building code/zoning and accessibility issues

title: The Art/Architecture of Packaging
description: Three hours lecture . Investigations into theories, techniques, and procedures of packaging (with emphasis on portfolio design) through traditional, mechanical,, and digital means

title: Assemblages
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 2546 and ARC 2723 ). Two hours lecture and two hours laboratory. Building construction assemblies in relation to building codes, zoning, structure, cladding, moisture/thermal barriers, and fenestration is explored through research and preliminary construction drawings

title: Environmental Building Systems II
description: (Prerequisites: ARC majors- ARC 2546 and ARC 2713 ; BCS majors- BCS 2713 and BCS 2116 ; others-consent of instructor).  Three hours lecture.  Concentrates on defining the mechanical and electrical (active) techniques available to architects for integrating thermal comfort and life safety into the built form. (Same as BCS 3723 )

title: Study Abroad Seminar I
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1213 or consent of instructor.) Three hours seminar. Six weeks of on-site instruction in Italy as part of the CAAD Italy study abroad program. Course content will vary to reflect the expertise of the instructor (Same as ART 3813 and ID 3813 .)

title: Study Abroad Seminar II
description: (Prerequisite: ART 1213 or consent of instructor.) Three hours seminar. Six weeks of on-site instruction in Italy as part of the CAAD Italy study abroad program. Course content will vary to reflect the expertise of the instructor (Same as ART 3823 and ID 3823 .)

title: Architectural Structures I
description: (Prerequisite: MA 1613 or MA 1713 and either ARC 1546 or BCS 1126 ) Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Application of the principles of statics and the strength of materials on structural elements. (Same as BCS 3904 )

title: Structures II
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 3904 ) Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Design and analysis of structural elements as part of frames and other structural systems. (Same as BCS 3914 )

title: Contemporary Philosophy and Architecture
description: (Prerequisites: Junior standing or permission of instructor). Three hours lecture. An examination of modernism and postmodernism in philosophy and architecture. (Same as PHI 4013 /6013)

description: (Prerequisite: ARC 3546 or equivalent or consent of director). One hour lecture. Eleven hours laboratory. Design of architectural elements integrating building systems, social concerns, and environmental factors. Studies involve intermediate to large scale projects in realistic architectural situations

title: Architectural Design IV-B
description: (Prerequisites: ARC 3914 and ARC 4536 or equivalent with approval of director). One hour lecture. Eleven hours laboratory. Integrated design studio that demonstrates design, materials and methods synthesis. Topics Include: user and regulatory requirements, site context, accessibility, building envelope assemblies, structural systems, environmental controls, life safety, and sustainability

description: Three hours lecture.Exploration of physical and virtual worlds from a theoretical, technical, communication, and design perspective

description: (Prerequisite Junior Standing or permission of instructor) Three hours seminar. This seminar course introduces students to the achievements, contributions, and history of women in the male-dominated professions of architecture, interior design, graphic design, interior design, and urban design. (Same as GS 4643 /6643)

title: Site Planning for Architects
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 2546 or by permission of the instructor) Three hours lecture.  Introduces natural ecological systems as they relate to human impacts.  The course also explores issues of Site Engineering and Grading, Urban Planning, Legal and Regulatory Context, Site sensitive site planning and stormwater management systems

description: Three hours lecture. Investigation and research regarding architectural issues including architectural law, contracts, litigation, case studies and other topical issues

description: One hour lecture. Six hours laboratory. Advanced study of analytical and intuitive methods of programming, leading to development of terminal project program to be used in ARC 5589

title: Architectural Practice
description: Three hours lecture. Investigation into issues facing the graduate architect including: responsibilities to the community and the profession; project and business management; client relations; and delivery of services

title: Architectural Design V-A
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 4546 ). One hour lecture. Fifteen hours laboratory. Theory and application of architectural problems at urban scale. Investigation of social, economic, political issues effecting architectural programming and design

title: Architectural Design V-B
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 5576 and ARC 5443 ). One hour lecture. Eleven hours laboratory. Development of architectural project of complex and comprehensive nature. Emphasis upon thorough examination of all aspects of building

(Same as GS 4643 /6643)

title: Public Design Seminar I
description: (Prerequisite: Acceptance in Public Design Inter Program.) Three hours lecture. Public practice theory; limitations of standard practice to meet contemporary social, economic and environmental needs; values and leadership of community organizations; examples of alternative practice

title: Public Design Seminar II
description: (Prerequisite: ARC 6813 .) Three hours lecture. Understanding community; local services and economic problems and global environmental risks; understanding minority subcultures, poverty, and the role of non-profit organizations

description: (Prerequisite: ARC 6823 .) Three hours lecture. Creating and using tools of public practice to help communities address social, economic and environmental problems; leadership skills, advocacy planning, sustaining a non-profit practice

description: (Prerequisite: ARC 6863 .) Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Challenging the status-quo; presentations by ten visionary people. Parallel studio team projects

description: Four hour lecture. Advanced exploration of digital input and output methods using 1,2,3,4, and 5D modeling software/hardware application in both virtual and physical problems in  theoretical and applied design and research projects

description: Four hours laboratory. Advanced laboratory providing exploration of digital input and output methods using 1,2,3,4 and 5D modeling software/hardward applications in both virtural and physical problems in theoretical and applied design and research projects

description: Four hours lecture. Provides the student with general grounding in the process of research, problem identification writing, and development of a formal argument in design and architecture

description: (Prerequisite: ARC 6633 ) Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Exploration of media and interaction design solutions through case studies and design exercises using emerging technologies and congruent design concepts

description: (Prerequisite: Consent of the instructor). One hour lecture. Four hours laboratory. Customization of existing software/production tools for the transformation of a script into computer graphics imagery
