description: (Prerequisite: EN 1103 and EN 1113 or their equivalent and Junior standing or consent of instructor).Three hours lecture. Introduction to prose of the legal profession, emphasizing rhetorical strategy and style. Advanced composition, including work with contracts, letters, regulations, memoranda of law, and briefs

title: Composition Pedagogy
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or Consent of Instructor).Three hours lecture. Introduction to practices and debates in college compositon pedogogies. Develops practical strategies for instruction in composition;introduces historical and theoretical scholarship in rhetoric and composition

title: Craft of Poetry
description: (Prerequisite: EN 3803 or consent of instructor).  (Graduate students may take EN 6303 for credit twice, even if they have already taken EN 4303 as undergraduates).  Three hours lecture.  The craft and practice of writing poetry

title: Craft of Fiction
description: (Prerequisite: EN 3903 or consent of instructor).  (Graduate students may take EN 6313 for credit twice, even if they have already taken EN 4313 as undergraduates).  Three hours lecture. The craft and practice of writing fiction

title: Literary Criticism from Plato-Present
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. A survey of literary criticism from Plato to the present

title: Southern Literature
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. A survey of southern literature from the antebellum period to the “post southern” present. Features selected works representing the diverse literary heritage of the U.S. South

title: Studies in African American Literature
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of English requirements in the student’s major). Three hours lecture. A study of selected authors and/or topics in African American literature. (Same as AAS 4343 )

title: Critical Theory Since 1900
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of English requirements in the student's major). Three hours lecture. A study of major strategies of interpretation since 1900, including psychoanalysis, Marxism, structuralism, feminism, deconstruction

title: Studies in Global Anglophone Literatures
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. A study of selected authors and/or topics in literature in English from around the world, especially Africa, Asia, and the Caribbean, and focusing on literary representations of histories of colonialism, migration, transnationalism, and globalization

title: English Literature and the World before 1800
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. A study of works of English literature and culture from before 1800 which engage with the non-European world, with a focus on the development of English identity through contact with diverse cultural forms

title: Digital Ethnic Studies
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. This course introduces ethnic studies approaches to studying literature, media, and digital culture with hands-on work on data literacy and digital storytelling

title: Postcolonial Literature and Theory
description: (Prerequisite:  Completion of English requirements in the student’s major).  Three hours lecture.  A critical introduction to postcolonial studies, examining the literatures of colonized or previously colonized peoples and their diasporas.  (Same as AAS 4393 )

title: Introduction to Linguistics
description: Three hours lecture. The descriptive and historical study of language; linguistic analysis and comparisons; language classification; language in its social and cultural setting. (Same as AN 4403 /6403 and LIN 4/6403)

title: History of the English Language
description: (Prerequisite: Twelve hours of English). Three hours lecture. The origin and development of the English language; past and ongoing changes in sounds and structure; influence of social history on language variation and change. Same as LIN 4413 /6413

title: Approaches to TESOL
description: Three hours lecture. This course covers various approaches to language teaching, including course design, classroom management, and sociocultural and sociopolitical issues surrounding being a language teacher

title: English Syntax
description: (Prerequisites: Either AN/EN/ LIN 4403 /6403, or Instructor Consent). Three hours lecture. Grammatical analysis of English with emphasis on pedagogical applications to teaching English as a foreign/second language. (Same as LIN 4443 /6443)

title: Methods in TESOL
description: Three hours lecture. This course covers the various practical pedagogical approaches common in TESOL including methods for teaching reading, listening, speaking, and writing as well as communicative approaches

title: Studies in Second Language Acquisition
description: (Prerequisite: AN/EN/ LIN 4403 /6403 or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. A survey of the major theories of language acquisition, concentrating on accounts of second language acquisition. (Same as FL/ LIN 4463 /6463)

title: Phonetics
description: (Prerequisites:  Either EN 4403 /6403, AN 4403 /6403, or Instructor Consent).  Three hours lecture.  This course focuses on the physical and linguistic aspects of speech sounds, including how they are produced, transcribed, measured, and perceived.  (Same as LIN 4473 /6473 and PSY 4473 /6473)

title: Shakespeare
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Shakespeare's plays through 1599

title: Shakespeare
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Shakespeare's plays from 1600

title: Chaucer
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of English requirements in the student's major). Three hours lecture. Studies in the major works of Chaucer. Readings in Middle English

title: Milton
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. The principal writings of Milton, including all of PARADISE LOST and PARADISE REGAINED, and some of the chief prose works

title: Language and Culture
description: Three hours lecture. Examination of language as a part of culture, a source of knowledge about other aspects of culture, and a social behavior. (Same as AN 4623 /6623, LIN 4623 /6623, and SO 4623 /6623)

title: Language and Society
description: Three hours lecture. Examination of relationship between language and society. How language varies regionally and socially; people’s use of and attitudes toward different ways of speaking. (Same as AN 4633 /6633, LIN 4633 /6633, and SO 4633 /6633)

title: The Nineteenth-Century British Novel
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. A study of the major nineteenth-century British novelists

title: British and Irish Novel Since 1900
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. A study of British and Irish novelists from Conrad and Woolf to Rushdie and Byatt, as well as literary movements including modernism, postmodernism, and postcolonialism

title: English Literature of the Sixteenth Century
description: (Prerequisites: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Study of the development of the English literary tradition, including works by Wyatt, Sidney, Spenser, Marlowe and others in their cultural and historical contexts

title: English Literature of the Seventeenth Century
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Study of major works of poetry, prose, and drama, including works by Donne, Jonson, Wroth and others in their literary, cultural, and historical contexts

title: British Literature and Culture of the Eighteenth Century
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. An exploration of important literary, political and cultural phenomena from the British eighteenth century. Covers a variety of genres

title: Postcolonial Literatures and Colonial Precursors
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. A study of how contemporary global postcolonial literatures take up works of literature in English from before 1900 to focus on relationships between colonialism, imperialism, and their aftermaths

title: Undergraduate Research in English
description: Hours, credits and deliverables to be arranged. The purpose of this course is to provide a student with the opportunity to participate in research and/or creative project beyond the traditional undergraduate experience, while allowing the university to track undergraduate participation in these activities

title: Types of Drama Since 1900
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Study of modern American, British, and Continental drama

title: The World Novel Since 1900
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Major world novelist since 1900, excluding British, Irish, and American

title: Poetry Since 1900
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Chief American and British poets; their verse technique and their contribution to poetic art

title: The Short Story
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ) Three hours lecture. A study of the short story genre

title: Victorian Poets and Prose Writers
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Intensive study of Tennyson, Browning, Arnold, Swinburne, and other Victorian poets, along with some of the non-fiction prose of the period

title: American Literature to 1800
description: (Prerequisite:  Completion of English requirements in the student’s major).  Three hours lecture.  Studies of topics in American literature from its beginnings to 1800

title: Nineteenth-Century U.S. Literature
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Studies of topics in U.S. literature from 1800-1900

title: American Novel Since 1900
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. A study of the American novel since Dreiser

title: Film Theory
description: Three hours lecture. Two hours lab. This course will introduce students to major theoretical positions and modes of analysis used to understand the various frameworks in which to view, criticize, analyze, and (re)contextualize film. (Same as ART 4924 and CO 4924 /6924)

title: Survey of Contemporary Literature
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Significant trends in European and American literature since the outbreak of World War II

title: Form and Theory of Fiction
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Theoretical aspects of fictional technique, genre, style; readings include novels, short stories, and writings about the craft of fiction. Recommended complement to creative writing courses

title: Form and Theory of Poetry
description: (Prerequisite: EN 1113 or EN 1173 ). Three hours lecture. Poetic theory; formal conventions, techniques, and innovations in the tradition of English and American poetry. Recommended complement to creative writing courses

title: Special Topics in English:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: First Year Seminar
description: One hour lecture. First-year seminars explore a diverse array of topices that provide students with an opportunity to learn about a specific discipline from skilled faculty members

title: Physical Geography Laboratory
description: Two hours laboratory. Lab course for GR 1113 but may be scheduled without GR 1113 . Laboratory exploring the study of the different components of the earth system and their interactions, as well as their locations on Earth

title: Physical Geography
description: Three hours lecture. Systematic study of the elements of the environmental process that form and characterize the earth's natural landscapes. May be taken as a science elective

title: Introduction to World Geography
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of the world's regions, with emphasis upon locational aspects, physical and cultural diversity, and environmental issues

title: Weather and Climate Laboratory
description: Two hours laboratory. Laboratory for GR 1133 but may be scheduled without GR 1133 . Includes the study of atmospheric composition and structure, atmospheric motion and forces, atmospheric moisture, organized weather systems, weather forecasting, and global climates

title: Weather and Climate
description: Three hours lecture. Descriptive study of weather with the objective of gaining appreciation of the variety of atmospheric phenomena. Explanation of daily weather events, their causes and impacts

title: Introduction to Climate and Climate Change
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of climate science with a focus on contemporary climate change, paleoclimate, climate impacts, and climate policies

title: Human Geography
description: Three hours lecture. Introduction to human geographic study of social, cultural, political and economic systems. Emphasis on concepts of space, place, landscape and nature/society relations

title: Maps and Remote Sensing
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Fundamental principles of cartography and remote sensing, including types and applications. Attention is given to interpretation of surface features, environmental problem solving, and environmental planning

title: Special Topics in Geography:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Weather Analysis
description: (Prerequisite: GR 1604).  Two hours laboratory.  Introduction to advanced meteorological analysis concepts including upper air map analysis, sounding analysis, numerical weather prediction, and basic synoptic meteorology analysis concepts

title: Conservation of Natural Resources
description: Three hours lecture. Consideration of the current problems associated with the conservation of soils, forests, waters, minerals, and wildlife in the United States and the world

title: Introduction to Meteorological Data Analysis & Visualization
description: Three hours lecture. An introduction to meteorological data analysis and visualization using Python. No previous coding experience is necessary. Designed for Geoscience majors

title: Survey of Geospatial Technologies
description: (Prerequisite: GR 2313 ). Three hours lecture. Geographic Information Systems, Remote Sensing and Global Positioning Systems applied to earth systems and science. Includes field excursions for hands on experience with current technologies

title: Lecture in Broadcast Meteorology
description: Three-hour lecture. Detailed examination of the major elements of the field of broadcast meteorology, with emphasis on career management, instilling public confidence, and work-life balance

title: Directed Individual Study in Geography:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Urban Geography
description: Three hours lecture. Historic trends in distribution and growth of urban settlements, urban location theory; economic bases, functions, and structure of cities and metropolitan areas; urban problems; planning

title: Political Ecology: Space, Nature, and Justice
description: Three hours lecture. This course provides an introduction to political ecology, an interdisciplinary approach to understanding human-environmental relations and the unequal causes and consequences of environmental change. Themes include the uneven ecological dynamics of development, resource extraction, and conservation, and the geographical dimensions of environmental justice and sustainability

title: Geography of North America
description: Three hours lecture. A regional survey of the United States and Canada with emphasis upon place names, physical landscapes, historical settlement patterns, cultural regions, and environmental issues

title: Geography of Latin America
description: Three hours lecture. A regional survey of Latin America with emphasis upon place names, physical environments, cultural landscapes and their evolution, and environmental issues

title: Geography of Europe
description: Three hours lecture. A regional survey of Europe with emphasis upon placements, physical environments, cultural landscapes, geopolitical evolution, and environment issues

title: Geography of Asia
description: Three hours lecture. A regional survey of Asia with emphasis upon placenames, physical geography, cultural diversity and cultural landscapes, geopolitical conflicts, and environmental issues

title: Geography of Russia and the Former Soviet Republics
description: Three hours lecture. A regional survey of the former Soviet Union republics with emphasis upon placenames, physical environments, ethnic diversity, geopolitical evolution, and environmental issues

title: Geography of Africa
description: Three hours lecture. A regional survey of the African continent with emphasis upon placenames, physical geography, cultural diversity and cultural landscapes, geopolitical changes, and environmental issues

title: Geography of the South
description: Three hours lecture. A regional survey of the South with emphasis upon physical and cultural landscapes, spatial processes, economic development, environmental issues, and racial justice. (Same as AAS 4263 )

title: Geography of Islamic World
description: A regional survey of Islamic countries of the world with emphasis upon physical landscapes, cultural landscapes and their evolution, geopolitical conflicts and environmental issues

title: Caribbean Geography
description: Three hours lecture. A regional survey of the West Indies, including the Greater Antilles, Lesser Antilles, and the Bahamas with emphasis on the physical, historical, demographic, political, cultural, economic, and environmental geographies

title: Principles of GIS
description: Two hours lecture and two hours laboratory. Spatial analysis and topological relationships of geographic data using Geographic Information Systems, with emphasis on GIS theory

title: Advanced GIS
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4303 /6303 or consent of instructor). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Vector-based file structure and GIS queries using spatial and geodatabases attributes. Descriptive and prescriptive modeling in the raster domain including regression and linear weighted modeling

title: Cartographic Sciences
description: (Prerequisite: Junior or graduate standing or consent of instructor.) Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Principles of cartographic theory and map design. Types of maps, map projections, proportional symbols, use of color, mapping and statistics, interactive maps, and map animation

title: Remote Sensing of the Physical Environment
description: Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Examines remote sensing methods applicable to large-area analyses of watershed-level drainage systems, urban landscape, landscape vegetation metrics, physical landscape structural components and atmospheric features

title: Advanced Remote Sensing in Geosciences
description: (Prerequisite: Either GR 4333 /6333, ECE 4423 /6423, or FO 4452/6452). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Geospatial image analysis; Theoretical basis of radiative transfer in atmosphere and water column; Quantitative remote sensing techniques and geospatial product development

title: Geodatabase Design
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4303 /6303). Three hours lecture. Examination of Geodatabase structures. Integration of relational databases with Geographic Information Systems. Management of spatial data using geodatabases. Implementation of Geodatabase processes through spatial programming

title: Geographic Information Systems Programming
description: (Prerequisite: Either GR 4303 /6303 or consent of instructor). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Design and implementation of geoprocessing scripts. Incorporation of modeling languages within geographic information systems (GIS) analysis. Seamless integration of other software programs with GIS software

title: Web GIS
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4303 /6303). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Primary conceptions and principles of Web GIS. Web GIS Apps. Online spatial modeling. Online big data analysis. Online 2D and 3D geovisualization. Geoinformatics services

title: Aerial Drone Applications in Geosciences
description: (Prerequisite: GR4333/6333 or GR4303/6303 or consent of instructor). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. The course covers remote sensing principles for small Uncrewed Aerial Systems (UASs) and sensor system, including radiometric and geometric corrections, image processing, data management, analysis, and visualization for land and water monitoring and management

title: Remote Sensing Seminar
description: (Prerequisite:Junior Standing). One hour lecture. Lectures by remote sensing experts from industry, academia, and governmental agencies on the next- generation systems, applications, and economic and societal impact of remote sensing. May be repeated for credit up to four credits. (Same as PSS 4411 /6411, ECE 4411 /6411, FO 4411 /6411)

title: Weather Forecasting I
description: (Prerequisites: GR 3011 and GR 4733 /6733). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Introduction to the process of creating and disseminating weather forecasts. Use of current weather data in creating daily forecasts for the local area

title: Weather Forecasting II
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4423 /6423). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Continuation of Weather Forecasting I. Emphasis placed on disseminating both oral and written forecasts for the local area as well as forecasting unique regional weather

title: Weather Prediction I
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4713 ). Three hours lecture. Weather analysis and forecasting. Emphasis on local, short-term forecasting techniques, including temperature forecasting, precipitation forecasting, and convective forecasting

title: Weather Prediction II
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4443 or consent of instructor). Three hours video and online. Continuation of GR 4443 . Case studies of weather forecasts. Emphasis on special weather events and places

title: Numerical Weather Prediction
description: This course provides students with an overview of the theory, processes, developments and applications of existing numerical weather prediction platforms

title: Practicum in Broadcast Meteorology I
description: (Prerequisite: GR 1603). One hour lecture. Two hours laboratory. Introduction to developing a weather story with emphasis on producing weather graphics for television, chroma key mechanics, and weathercast communication

title: Practicum in Broadcast Meteorology II
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4502 /6502). One hour lecture. Two hours laboratory. Continuation of Practicum in Broadcast Meteorology I with emphasis on weather graphics production, weathercast performance, image, and communication. Supported by lab practice

title: Practicum in Broadcast Meteorology III
description: Prerequisite: GR 4512 /6512. One hour lecture. Two hours laboratory. Emphasis on advanced weathercasting, including field reporting, severe weather, and building graphics. Students are assigned actual television weather shows, with performance emphasis in the lab

title: Practicum in Broadcast Meteorology IV
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4522 /6522).One hour lecture.Two hours laboratory. Emphasis on the weathercasting job market in television. Students create actual television weather shows, and focus on producing a resume tape during the semester

title: Computer Methods in Meteorology
description: (Prerequisite: GR 1603).  Two hours lecture, two hours lab.  Overview of computational methods and techniques commonly used in operational meteorology, focusing on scientific visualization and analysis, and numerical weather prediction

title: Aviation Meteorology
description: (Prerequisite: GR 1604). Three hours lecture. Overview of meteorological concepts important to the aviation community, including how relevant weather data are collected and disseminated and how atmospheric properties relate to the basic physics of flight and aircraft performance

title: Climatology
description: (Prerequisite: GR 1114 or GR 1123 ). Three hours lecture. Study of the elements and controls of weather and climate, distribution and characteristics of climatic regions

title: Applied Climatology
description: (Prerequisites: GR 1603) Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Problem solving in today's world in topics such as bioclimatology, agricultural climatology and land use climatology

title: Physical Meteorology
description: (Prerequisite:GR 1603). An investigation of cloud physics/precipitation processes and solar/terrestrial radiation, including atmospheric dynamics, atmospheric electricity, optics, and instrumentation

title: Statistical Climatology
description: (Prerequisites: GR 1603 or GG 1113 and MA 1313 or MA 1713 ). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. A survey of the types of statistical weather data available. Manipulation of the data on various temporal and spatial scales

title: Meteorological Internship:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credits to be arranged. Internship with television station, private company or government agency under supervision of instructor

title: Physical Meteorology and Climatology I
description: (Prerequisite: GR 1604 and MA 1323 ). Three hours lecture. An investigation of the physical aspects of Earth’s climate, including interactions between the atmosphere, hydrosphere, and land surface, and how they are affected by climate variability and change

title: Physical Meteorology and Climatology II
description: (Prerequisite: MA 1713 and GR 4643 ). Three hours lecture.  An investigation into important physical meteorology concepts, including introductory atmospheric thermodynamics, the planetary boundary layer, and cloud and moisture physics with an emphasis on meteorological theory and applications

title: Synoptic Meteorology I
description: (Prerequisites: GR 1603 or equivalent.) Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Fundamental principles behind weather forecasting. Physical processes in the atmosphere, atmospheric circulation systems, air mass analysis, frontogenesis and frontolysis

title: Synoptic Meteorology
description: (Prerequisite:GR 1603 and MA 1713 ) Three hour lecture. Principles and derivation of meteorological theory. Emphasis on energy exchanges, atmospheric moisture, physical processes of atmospheric motion, air masses and fronts, and cyclogenesis

title: Satellite and Radar Meteorology
description: (Prerequisite: GR 1603.) Three hours lecture. Study of the history, the operations, and the applications of satellites and radar in weather analysis. Theory of meteorological measurements in determinations of atmospheric structure

title: Satellite Meteorology
description: (Prerequisites: GR 4733 , GR 4643 ). Two hours lecture, two hours laboratory. Overview of remote sensing methods and techniques commonly used in satellite meteorology, focusing on physical mechanisms, atmospheric image analysis, and real-time weather applications

title: Natural Hazards and Processes
description: (Prerequisites: GR 1114). Three hours lecture. A survey of natural phenomena in geology, oceanography and astronomy as applied to meteorology. Detailed study of earthquakes, volcanoes, ocean movements, and solar activity

title: Dynamic Meteorology I
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4733 /6733 and MA 1723 ). Three hours lecture. In-depth examination of theoretical methods for determining atmospheric stability and the tools necessary to interrogate the vertical profile of the atmosphere

title: Observations of Severe Local Storms
description: One hour field experience. Real-world practice in forecasting, nowcasting observation, and reporting of severe storms in U.S. Great Plains

title: Forecasting Severe Local Storms
description: One hour lecture and two hours lab. This course provides a theoretical overview and practical application of the severe local storms forecasting process

title: Field Methods of Severe Local Storms
description: Two hours lecture. One hour field experience. Application of the latest synoptic and mesoscale severe weather forecasting methods concluding with field operations in the U.S. Great Plains

title: Forensic Geoscience
description: (Prerequisite: GG 1113 , GR 1114 or GR 1604). Three hours lecture. Multidisciplinary study using all branches of geoscience in investigating criminal offenses, reconstructing accidents and as evidence in civil and criminal court cases

title: Radar Meteorology
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4733 .)  Two hours lecture.  Two hours lab.  Study of the history, the operation, and the application of radar in weather analysis.  Theory and application of radar measurements in the determination of meteorological threats

title: Thermodynamic Meteorology
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4733 /6733 or GR 4713 /6713). Three hours lecture. Examination of the meteorological stability within the earth's atmosphere. Focus on analysis of the various stability indices related to predicting severe weather

title: Severe Weather
description: (Prerequisites: GR 4913 /6913). Three hours lecture. Descriptive study of severe and unusual weather across the earth.  Explanation of variations in severe weather in both spatial and temporal scales

title: Dynamic Meteorology II
description: Three hours lecture. (Prerequisite GR 4823 /6823 and MA 2733 ) Quantitative analysis and consideration of atmospheric circulation including jet streams, mid-latitude cyclones, vorticity and atmospheric kinetics

title: Tropical Meteorology
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4733 ). Three hours lecture. Topics include the dynamics and circulation of the tropical atmosphere, characteristics of tropical cyclones, and forecasting methodologies for tropical weather

title: Mesoscale Meteorology
description: (Prerequisite: GR 4733 or GR 4713 ). Three hours lecture. Descriptive and physical understanding of Mesoscale processes and their relevance to the synoptic environment. A strong focus will be placed upon Severe Local Storms

title: Special Topics in Geography:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Earth Sciences I Laboratory
description: Two hours laboratory. Laboratory for GG 1113 , but may be scheduled without GG 1113 . Includes study of earth materials, maps, and aerial photographs. Planned primarily as a science elective for the non-geology major

title: Survey of Earth Sciences I
description: Three hours lecture. Study of the Earth in space, the materials of which the Earth is composed, and the processes affecting change on the Earth. Planned primarily  as a science elective for the non-geology major

title: Earth Sciences II Laboratory
description: Two hours laboratory. Laboratory for GG 1123 , but may be scheduled without GG 1123 . Includes the study of fossils, geologic maps, and geologic cross sections. Planned primarily as a science elective for the non-geology major

title: Survey of Earth Sciences II
description: (Prerequisite: GG 1113 ). Three hours lecture. Origin and development of the Earth through geologic time. Planned primarily as a science elective for the non-geology major

title: Planetary Geology
description: Three hours lecture. Process oriented examination of the planets and their satellites with emphasis on the "Earth-like" planets and moons

title: Special Topics in Geosciences:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Introduction to Environmental Geology
description: (Prerequisite: GG 1113 ). Three hours lecture. Consideration of those aspects of earth science concerned with problems arising from intensive use of earth by modern society

title: Introduction to Oceanography
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of the basic principles and applications of science to the study of the marine environment

title: Water Resources
description: Three hours lecture. Introduction to the location, use, recovery and environmental problems of surface and subsurface waters

title: Directed Individual Study in Geosciences:  1-6 hours.
description: (Prerequisite: Junior standing). Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Resources and the Environment
description: Three hours lecture. Formation and development of natural resources involving the basic evolution, planning, and design of a typical lignite coal mine, including environmental monitoring and reclamation

title: Earth and Atmospheric Energy Resources
description: Three hours lecture. Formation, deposition, and extraction of fossil fuel resources, including coal, conventional, and unconventional hydrocarbons.  Introduction to the geologic, geographic, and climatic aspects of Earth and atmospheric-sourced renewable resources

title: Micropaleontology
description: (Prerequisite: GG 1123 ). Three hours lecture. A study of microscopic fossils. May be taken with GG 4201

title: Mineralogy
description: (Prerequisites: GG 1113 and CH 1223 ). Three hours lecture. Three hours laboratory. The physical and chemical properties of minerals; crystallography, origin, distribution, association, uses, and identification of minerals

title: Petrology
description: (Prerequisite: GG 4114 ). Three hours lecture and two hours laboratory. An investigation of important petrological concepts, including magmatic differentiation, classification of igneous and metamorphic rocks, and interpretation of thin sections of the rocks

title: Principles of Paleoecology
description: (Prerequisite: GG 1123 ). Three hours lecture. A study of paleoecology with special emphasis on marine paleoecology. May be taken with GG 4201

title: Engineering Geology
description: Three hours lecture. Application of geologic principles to location and construction of engineering structures; engineering properties of geologic materials; engineering application of equipment used by geologists

title: Practicum on Paleontology
description: (Prerequisites: GG 1123 and GG 1121 ). One hour lecture. Two hours laboratory. Laboratory for GG 4203 but may instead be taken with GG 4113 or GG 4133 . A practicum in morphology of fossils, biostratigraphy, and paleoecology

title: Principles of Paleobiology
description: (Prerequisites: GG 1123 ). Three hours lecture. Three hours laboratory. An introductory study of topics in paleobiology. May be taken with GG 4201

title: Applied Geophysics
description: (Prerequisite: PH 1113 or PH 2213 ). Three hours lecture. A survey of the basic principles and applications of geophysics with major emphasis on petroleum exploration

title: Principles of Sedimentary Deposits I
description: (Prerequisite: GG 1113 ). Three hours lecture. Three hours laboratory. Treatment of sediment and sedimentary rock. Emphasis on texture, fluid processes, deposition, structure, and diagenesis; stratigraphic analysis; and application to subsurface flow systems

title: Karst Processes and Landforms
description: (Prerequisite: GG 1113 ). Three hours lecture. Processes of dissolution and the formation of Karst, pseudokarst features and landscapes. Major impact of diagenesis on rocks, landscape evolution and related subsurface hydrology

title: Geowriting
description: Three hours lecture.  Prepares students to present geosciences information through research papers and other forms of professional communication.  Emphasizes writing for careers or advanced study in the geosciences

title: Gulf Coast Stratigraphy
description: (Prerequisite: GG 4304 ). Three hours lecture or field trips. Systematic study of the stratigraphy of the Gulf Coast; actual field experience substituted for class work when conditions permit

title: Structural Geology
description: (Prerequisites: GG 4114 ). Three hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Application of the principles of mechanics to the forces deforming the rocks of the Earth's crust; emphasis on structures in sedimentary rocks

title: Subsurface Methods
description: One hour lecture. Four hours laboratory. The study of subsurface geologic methods including contouring, sampling study, various types of logging, and the interpretation of subsurface data

title: Principles of Sedimentary Deposits II
description: (Prerequisite: GG 4304 ). Three hours lecture. Application of principles from GG 4304 . Introduces facies associations produced in depositional environments, systems, and systems tracts, tectonics and sedimentation, basin classification, and sequence analysis

title: Summer Geology Field Camp
description: (Prerequisites: GG 4413, GG 4443 , and GG 4124 ). Three hours lecture and three hours lab. Geologic maps, stratigraphic columns, structural cross-sections and reports will be prepared based on field data collected by the student

title: Geomorphology
description: Three hours lecture. The origin and characteristics of land forms based on a consideration of geologic processes, stages of development, and geological structure

title: Coastal Environments
description: Three hours lecture. An introduction to world coastal environments, with emphasis upon major shoreline-shaping processes, geographical variation in coastal landforms, human impacts, and environmental concerns

title: Geosciences Study Abroad
description: Three hours study abroad. Identification of landforms and geomorphic processes and the field data collection techniques. Emphasis on human-environmental interactions

title: Community Engagement in Environmental Geosciences
description: Three hours lecture. Research topics, field practices, and community engagement opportunities related to Environmental Geosciences. Activities include citizen science contributions and design; local field experiences to identify and evaluate various environmental concerns impacting communities; conduct community environmental sustainability evaluations; and develop environmental geosciences-focused community engagement events

title: Physical Hydrogeology
description: Three hours lecture. Advanced study of the interrelationship of ground water and its geologic environment with emphasis on occurrence, distribution, and movement

title: Chemical Hydrogeology
description: (Prerequisite: GG 4613 ). Three hours lecture. Advanced study of groundwater and its environment with emphasis on the chemical interaction of water with porous solids and the transport of chemical constituents

title: Introduction to Geochemistry
description: (Prerequisite: CH 1223 and GG 1113 ). Three hours lecture.  Survey of fundamental geochemical principles and methods.  Learning in this course will be achieved by participation in analysis of published or unpublished datasets with further interpretation and application to the natural systems

title: Structural Geology for Industry Applications
description: Two hours lecture, two hours lab. Investigation into geomechanical models of the subsurface as they pertain to the development and failure of geological structures, with emphasis on the effect of structures and stresses in industrial drilling

title: Biogeochemical Evolution of Planet Earth
description: Three hours lecture. An introduction to the mechanisms and evolution of global biogeochemical cycles through Earth’s history, with emphasis on both fundamental principles and outstanding case studies

title: Special Topics in Geosciences:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: History of Science in Six Ideas
description: A survey of the historical context and consequences of six concepts (the body, the senses, humanity, nature, power, the cosmos) in the Western world

title: History of Technology in Six Objects
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of the history of technology in modern society through a study of six technological artifacts and systems

title: Early U.S. History
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of U.S. history through Reconstruction

title: Modern U.S. History
description: Three hours lecture. A continuation of HI 1063 , covering the period from Reconstruction to the present

title: World History Before 1500
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of world history since prehistory until about 1500

title: World History Since 1500
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of world history since about 1500 until the present

title: Early Western World
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of western world history from ancient times to about 1600

title: Modern Western World
description: Three hours lecture. A continuation of HI 1213 , covering the period from the 17th century to the present

title: East Asian Civilizations to 1300
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of China and Japan and their peoples through a multi-disciplinary approach from pre-history until the the thirteenth century

title: East Asian Civilizations since 1300
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of China and Japan and their peoples through a multi-disciplinary approach from 1300 to the present

title: Special Topics in History:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: African American History to 1865
description: Three hours lecture. An historical examination of the live and culture of African Americans in the United States from European colonization to the end of the Civil War. (Same as AAS 3013 )

title: African American History since 1865
description: Three hours lecture. An historical examination of the live and culture of African Americans in the United States from European colonization to the end of the Civil War. (Same as AAS 3013 )

title: History of U.S. Popular Culture
description: Three hours lecture. An historical analysis of the development of popular culture, related industries and their impact on American society

title: World Environmental History
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). A historical analysis of the interaction of humans and the natural world from the Neolithic period to the present

title: Mississippi History
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A survey of Mississippi history examining economic, social, political, geographical, and cultural aspects of the state's past

title: Delta History Service and Experiential Spring Break
description: (Prerequisite:  Consent of Instructor) One and a half hours lecture.  One and a half hours field experience.  Survey of historical and contemporary issues facing the Mississippi Delta region, with week-long service-learning trip to the Mississippi Delta during Spring Break

title: History of U.S. Transportation
description: Three hours lecture. Course examines the history of transportation in the United States from the colonial period to the present and its role in constructing an American identity

title: Gulf Coast History
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level course in History, Philosophy, Religion, or English; or permission of the instructor). Three hours lecture. A history of the American regions bordering the Gulf of Mexico from pre-contact to the present, concentrating on the environment, culture, and economy of the region

title: The Western Church: Beginning to Reformation
description: (Prerequisites: Completion of any 100-level course in history or philosophy and religion). Three hours lecture. An examination of the institutions, doctrines, and spirituality of the Western Church and their impact on Western European politics, society, and cluture. (Same as REL 3703 )

title: History of African American Women
description: Three hours lecture.  Examination of black women from their African origins to the present; emphasizes the social, economic and political engagement of women in American society, including reform movements, family life, business, and the arts.  (Same as AAS 3713 / GS 3713 )

title: History of England
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A survey of English history from its origins to the present

title: Hitler and Nazi Germany
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course. Three hours lecture. A study of Adolf Hitler's personality and rise to power; and examination of the theory and practice of National Socialism

title: History of the Holocaust
description: Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course or consent of the instructor). Three hours lecture. An examination of the role of perpetrators, victims, and bystanders during the Holocaust

title: Modern European Imperialism
description: Prerequisite: Completion of a 1000-level history course or consent of instructor. Three hours lecture. The course surveys European imperialism from 1815 to the post-colonial world

title: Modern Latin America
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000 level history course). Three hours lecture. An introduction to the modern history of the major Latin America nations and their importance to the United States

title: The United States and Latin America
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000 level history course). Three hours lecture. History of foreign policies and diplomatic relations in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries with an emphasis on strategic and security issues

title: 20th Century World History
description: (Prerequisite: completion of any 1000 level history course). Three hours lecture. Study of the world since 1900 concentrating on the themes of imperialism, nationalism, war, and industrialization

title: Historiography and Historical Method
description: (Prerequisites: Junior or Senior standing). Three hours lecture. The writings and interpretations of leading European and American historians, bibliographical aids, methods of research, preparation of bibliographies, practice in writing a research paper

title: Directed Individual Study in History:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Colonial America
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. Study of the earliest English settlements to 1740. Emphasis on Puritanism, interaction with other people, expansion and forming of societal and political institutions

title: Jacksonian America 1825 to 1850
description: (Prerequisites: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. America from the beginnings of the Jacksonian movement, its political, economic and social battles, through trans-continental expansion and the Mexican War

title: Civil War and Reconstruction 1850 to 1877
description: (Prerequisites: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. Origins of the secessionist movement and the Civil War, the political and military battles of the War, and the struggle to reunify the nation

title: Revolutionary America
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. American provinces from 1740 until 1783. Emphasis on maturation. pluralism, role in British empire, religion, Enlightenment, and causes, idealogy, and conduct of the Revolution

title: U.S. History 1877 to 1917
description: (Prerequisites: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A survey of political, economic, social, and constitutional developments

title: U.S. 1917 - 1945
description: (Prerequisites: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A study of all major aspects of American government and life through World War II

title: U.S. History since 1945
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A study of all major aspects of American government and life since the end of World War II

title: U.S. Economic History
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. An intensive study of economic change in the United States and its impact on political and social development. (Same as EC 4183 /6183)

title: U.S. Environmental History
description: (Prerequisite:Any 1000 level history course) Three hours lecture.  A survey of the impact of the environment in shaping the American culture,literature,politics, and economy from European colonization to the present

title: Diplomatic History of the U.S.
description: (Prerequisites: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A study of American foreign policy from the founding of the Republic to the present time

title: History of Grand Strategy & International Security
description: (Prerequisiste: Completion of any 1000 level history course) Three hours seminar. A discussion of the historic literature of grand strategy and key events in the history of international relations

title: Intelligence Gathering in the 20th Century
description: (Prerequisite:Completion of any 1000-level history course or consent of instructor). Three hour lecture. A discussion of myth/reality of intelligence gathering and its use as a military or diplomatic tool

title: American Military History
description: (Prerequisites: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A survey of the military history of the United States from colonial times to the present

title: American Life and Thought
description: Three hours lecture. A survey of the changing lives and ideas of Americans from colonial to modern times. Family life, religion, recreation, dress, communities, social theories, medicine

title: History of Religion in America
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000 level history course). Three hours lecture. Surveys history of religion in America, emphasizing interaction with social and political developments

title: America's Vietnam War
description: (Prerequisite:  Completion of any 1000-level history course).  Three hours lecture.  Analysis of the U.S. conduct of Vietnam War, such as:  Cold War context, presidential decision-making, military doctrine, domestic opposition, and legacy

title: Women in American History
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A study of the economic, political, and social activities of women in American history. Emphasis on Southern women

title: History of Southern Women
description: Three hours lecture. The lives and images of women in the South from colonial times to the present. Native-,African-, and European-American woman to be studied

title: History of Gender and Science
description: Three hours seminar. Historical survey of scientific research on sex, the role of gender in the culture of science, and the contributions of women to scientific practice

title: The Old South
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. Development of the Old South from colonization through the slavery controversy and the Civil War

title: The New South
description: (Prerequisites: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. Southern life from Reconstruction times to the present

title: The American West
description: (Prerequisites: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A survey of the western frontier in American history from colonial times to 1900

title: Native American History to 1830
description: (Prerequisite: completion of any 1000 level history course). Three hour lecture, Native American history at 1830, concentrating on the theme of survival and adaption to changes wrought by contact with Europeans

title: Immigration and Ethnicity in the United States
description: (Prerequisite:  Completion of any 1000-level history course).  Three hours lecture.  A history of American immigration from the colonial period to the present day.  Outlines immigration patterns, policies, and the immigrant experience

title: African American History and Culture
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course) Africian-Americans from their Africian origins to the present, emphasizing black-white relations in the making of America.(Same as AAS 4363 )

title: History of Modern Civil Rights Movement
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A history of the Black struggle for equality in the United States between 1930 and 1970. (Same as AAS 4373 )

title: Native American History Since 1830
description: (Prerequisite: completion of any 1000-level history course) Three hours lecture. Study of American Indian history to the present with emphasis on the loss of Indian autonomy and the struggles to regain it

title: Rural America
description: Examines the transformation and cultural significance of rural America from the colonial era to the early 21st century

title: The Ancient Near East
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A study of the origins and development of civilizations in Mesopotamia, Egypt, and Syria-Palestine from prehistoric times to the end of the Persian period. (Same as MEC 4403 /6403 and REL 4403 /6403)

title: Ancient Greece and Rome
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A survey of the civilization of ancient Greece and Rome

title: Greek History
description: Three hours lecture. Introduction to the history of ancient Greece from the Bronze Age through the height of the Greek city-states and the rise of the kingdom of Macedon. (Same as FL 4423 /6423)

title: Roman History
description: Three hours lecture. Introduction to the history of Rome from its foundations through the Republic, the Empire, and the ultimate decline in late antiquity. (Same as FL 4433 /6433)

title: Terrorism in America
description: Three hours lecture. Survey of the impact of domestic and international terrorism on American politics, society, and foreign policy since the Civil War

title: History of the Cold War
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. The political, social, cultural, and economic history of the Cold War, from its origins in the early twentieth century to its conclusion in 1991

title: Science and Technology to Newton
description: Three hours lecture. An examination of the history of science and technology from pre-history to Newton

title: China Since 1800
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. China's tumultuous centuries of imperial decline, foreign assault, and nationalist and communist revolutions. Cultural and social transformations and the quest for institutional and economic modernization

title: Japan Since 1600
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. Examines the major political, cultural, economic, military and diplomatic events that have brought Japan from sheltered feudalism to international preeminence

title: Medieval Civilization
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. An intensive study of medieval institutions and culture

title: History of the Soviet Union
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. The political, social, cultural and economic developement of the Soviet Union from its pre-Revolutionary origins to its collapse in 1991

title: Renaissance and Reformation
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. The Renaissance and its relation to religion, politics, and social life; origins of the Reformation movement and its effect on Europe in early modern times

title: History of Science and Technology
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. Science and technology from Newton to the present, emphasizing the relationship between scientific innovation and technological application

title: History of Artificial Intelligence
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A historical survey of the development of artificial intelligence, from early theories of automation to recent developments in machine learning

title: Europe, 1789-1914
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A study of the political, economic, and intellectual foundations of nineteenth century society

title: Europe: The First World War to Hitler
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. European development from the beginning of the First World War to the beginning of the Second World War

title: Europe: The Second World War to the Common Market
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. European development from the beginning of the Second World War to the present time

title: Tudor and Stuart England
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. The development of English institutions during the Tudor and Stuart periods

title: History of Britain Since 1688
description: Three hours seminar. Historical survey of Britian since 1688 with particular emphasis on political, economic and cultural change and relations between the component nationalities with the United Kingdom

title: War, Diplomacy, and Statecraft in Europe, 1648-1989
description: Three hours seminar. Historical survey of international politics since the eighteenth century within its economic, cultural, and military context

title: History of Russia
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course.) Three hours lecture. The political, social, cultural, and economic development of Russia from Kievan to Soviet times

title: History of Modern Germany
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. The history of German institutions in modern times

title: History of Modern France
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. The history of French institutions in modern times

title: African Civilization to 1880
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. This is a survey course which traces the major developments in Africa to 1880. (Same as AAS 4783 )

title: Modern Africa
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. This course traces Africa's history from 1880 to the present. It discusses how Africa lost and regained its sovereignty and the dilemma of independence. (Same as AAS 4793 )

title: Issues in Women's History
description: Three hours lecture. An analysis of major issues in American women's history

title: Modern Mexico
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. The political, economic, and social development of the Mexican nation from Independence through the age of dictators to the Great Revolution and its aftermath

title: Issues in African American History
description: Three hours lecture. An analysis of major issues in African American history

title: U.S. History of Medicine
description: Three hour lecture. Survey of the development of the medical profession and public health in the United States. Medical education and practice, scientific research, epidemics and illness emphasized

title: The Far East
description: (Prerequisite: Completion of any 1000-level history course). Three hours lecture. A study of the impact of western civilization on China, Japan, and India in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries

title: African Americans and the Law
description: Prerequisite:  Sophomore standing or higher ).Three hours lecture. Analysis of the legal and constitutional history of African Americans from the codification of slavery and discrimination in the North to the rise of segregation. (Same as AAS 4983 )

title: Special Topics in History:  1-9 hours.
description: (Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Intermediate Algebra
description: ( MA 0103 is designed to prepare a student for MA 1213 /1313) Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Real numbers, algebraic expressions, factoring, algebraic fractions, linear equations/inequalities, quadratic and rational equations, radicals. Required for students with an ACT math subscore of 17 or 18 that do not place into a higher course via the math department placement test and for all students with an ACT math subscore below 17. Does not count towards any degree

title: College Algebra Linked Lab- Corequisite Model
description: (Prerequisite: MACT 17 or 18 and ACT 20 or above). Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Review of fundamentals; linear and quadratic equations; inequalities; functions; simultaneous equations; topics in the theory of equations

title: Math in Your World
description: (Prerequisites: ACT Math Sub-score 19 or above or grade of C or better in MA 0103 ). Three hour lecture. Topics will include but are not limited to ratios & proportions, unit conversions, formula manipulation, logical reasoning, financial literacy, general number sense, and the use of Excel to solve real world problems

title: College Algebra
description: (Students with credit in MA 1713 will not receive credit for this course; Prerequisite: ACT math subscore 19, or grade of C or better in MA 0103 ). Two hour lecture. Two hours laboratory. Review of fundamentals; linear and quadratic equations; inequalities; functions; simultaneous equations; topics in the theory of equations

title: Trigonometry
description: (Students with credit in MA 1713 will not receive credit for this course; Prerequisite: ACT Math subscore 24 (or higher for some sections), or grade of C or better in MA 1103 or MA 1313 ). Three hours lecture. The trigonometric functions: identities; trigonometric equations; applications

title: Structure of the Real Number System
description: (Prerequisite: C or better in either MA 1103 or MA 1313 or an ACT Math sub-score of 24). Three hours lecture. The nature of mathematics; introductory logic; structure and development of the real number system. (For Elementary and Special Education majors only)

title: Problem Solving with Real Numbers
description: (Prerequisite: C or better in MA 1413 ). Three hours lecture. Proportions, percent problems, probability, counting principles,statistics. (For Elementary of Special Education majors only)

title: Informal Geometry and Measurement
description: (Prerequisites: C or better in both MA 1413 and MA 1423 ). Three hours lecture. Measurements and informal geometry. (For Elementary and Special Education majors only)

title: Precalculus
description: (Prerequisites: Math ACT 24 or C or better in MA 1103 or MA 1313 ). Three hours lecture. Properties, applications, and graphs of linear, quadratic, polynomial, exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric functions, identities, equations, and inverses. (Degree credit will not be granted for MA 1453 and either MA 1313 /1323)

title: Calculus for Business and Life Sciences I
description: (Prerequisite: ACT Math subscore 24, or grade of C or better in MA 1103 or MA 1313 ). Three hours lecture. Algebraic and some transcendental functions, solutions of systems of linear equations, limits, continuity, derivatives, applications

title: Calculus I
description: (Prerequisite: ACT Math subscore 26, or grade of C or better in 1323 or 1453). Three hours lecture. Analytic geometry; functions; limits; continuity; derivatives of algebraic and transcendental functions; applications of the derivative. Honors section available

title: Calculus II
description: (Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in MA 1713 ). Three hours lecture. Anti-differentiation; the definite integral; applications of the definite integral; integration of transcendental functions; other techniques of integration. Honors section available

title: Elementary Statistics
description: (Prerequisite: ACT Math subscore 24, or grade of C or better in MA 1103 or MA 1313 or MA 1213 ). Three hours lecture. Introduction to descriptive statistics, random variables, probability distributions, estimation, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing. Computer instruction for analysis. (Same as ST 2113 )

title: Calculus III
description: (Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in MA 1723 ). Three hours lecture. Parametric and Polar Equations; infinite series; introduction to vectors; vector functions. Honors section available

title: Calculus IV
description: (Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in MA 2733 ). Three hours lecture. Differential calculus of functions of several variables; multiple integration; vector calculus. Honors section available

title: Introduction to Modern Scientific Computing
description: (Prerequisite: MA 1713 or equivalent). Three hours lecture. Basic programming skills and applications to scientific computing; iteration and recursion; accuracy and efficiency issues; matrix operations; data interpolation; unconstrained optimization; regression analysis; multiple local minima problems. Prior programming experience is not required

title: Special Topics in Mathematics:  1-9 hours.
description: Credit and title to be arranged. This course is to be used on a limited basis to offer developing subject matter areas not covered in existing courses. (Courses limited to two offerings under one title within two academic years)

title: Foundations of Mathematics
description: (Prerequisite: MA 1723 ). Three hours lecture. The logical structure of mathematics; the nature of a mathematical proof; applications to the basic principles of algebra and calculus

title: Introduction to Linear Algebra
description: (Prerequisite: MA 1723 ). Three hours lecture. Basic principles of linear algebra; vector spaces; matrices; matrix algebra; linear transformations; systems of linear equations; eigenvalues and eigenvectors; orthogonality and Gram-Schmidt process

title: Introduction to Statistical Inference
description: (Prerequisite:ACT math subscore 24, or grade of C or better in MA 1313 ).  Two hours lecture. Two hours laboratory. Basic concepts and methods of statistics, including descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, sampling distribution,estimation,hypothesis testing, introduction to analysis of variance, simple linear regression. (Same as ST 3123 )

title: Introduction to Modern Algebra
description: (Prerequisite: MA 3113 and MA 3053 ). Three hours lecture. Rings,integral domains, and fields with special emphasis on the integers, rational numbers, real numbers and complex numbers; theory of polynomials

title: Differential Equations I
description: (Prerequisite: MA 2743 or coregistration in MA 2743 ). Origin and solutions of first and second order differential equations; Laplace Transform methods; applications

title: Differential Equations II
description: (Prerequisite: MA 3253 ). Three hours lecture. Systems of differential equations; matrix representations; infinite series solution of ordinary differential equations; selected special functions; boundary-value problems; orthogonal functions: Fourier series

title: Foundations of Geometry
description: (Prerequisite: MA 1723 and MA 3053 ) Three hours lecture. The structural nature of geometry; modern methods in geometry: finite geometrics

title: History of Mathematics
description: (Prerequisite: MA 2733 or coregistration in MA 2733 ). Three hours lecture. A historical development of mathematicians and their most important contributions will be emphasized

title: Directed Individual Study in Mathematics:  1-6 hours.
description: Hours and credits to be arranged

title: Discrete Mathematics
description: (Prerequisites: MA 3163 or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. Sets, relations, functions, combinatorics, review of group and ring theory, Burnside's theorem, Polya's counting theory, group codes, finite fields, cyclic codes, and error-correcting codes

title: Graph Theory
description: (Prerequisites: MA 3113 or consent of instructor). Three hours lecture. Basic concepts, graphs, and matrices, algebraic graph theory, planarity and nonplanarity, Hamiltonian graphs, digraphs, network flows, and applications

title: Matrices and Linear Algebra
description: (Prerequisites: MA 3113 and MA 3253 ). Three hours lecture. Linear transformations and matrices; eigen values and similarity transformations; linear functionals, bilinear and quadratic forms; orthogonal and unitary transformations; normal matrices; applications of linear algebra

title: Group Theory
description: (Prerequisite: MA 3163 or consent of the instructor). Three hours lecture. Elementary properties: normal subgroups; factor groups; homomorphisms and isomorphisms; Abelian groups; Sylow theorems; composition series; solvable groups

title: Number Theory
description: (Prerequisite: MA 3113 ). Three hours lecture. Divisibility: congruences; quadratic reciprocity; Diophantine equations; continued fractions

title: Mathematical Foundations of Machine Learning
description: (Prerequisite: MA 2743 and MA 3113 ). Three hours lecture. Basic machine learning principles and classifiers; gradient-based methods for optimization; data preprocessing techniques; feature selection methods; quadratic programming; Lagrange multipliers and duality; kernel methods; mathematics and applications for deep learning

title: Senior Seminar in Mathematics
description: (Prerequisites: MA 3163 and MA 3253 and MA 4633 ) Three hours lecture. Students explore topics in current mathematical research, write expository articles, and give oral presentations. Refinement of specialized writing skills needed for effective mathematical communication

title: Data Analysis I
description: (Prerequisite: MA 2743 . Corequisite: MA 3113 ). Three hours lecture. Data description and descriptive statistics, probability and probability distributions, parametric one-sample and two-sample inference procedures, simple linear regression, one-way ANOVA. Use of SAS. (Same as ST 4243 /6243)

title: Statistical Learning
description: (Prerequisite: MA/ ST 4243 /6243). Three hours lecture. This course is about concepts and the implementation of selected statistical and machine learning methods. This course includes predictive modeling, regression and classification problems, data handling for both quantitative and categorical types, model evaluation/selection, and relevant interpretation. (Same as ST 4253 /6253)

title: Numerical Analysis I
description: (Prerequisites: CSE 1233 or equivalent, MA 3113 , and MA 2743 ).  Three hours lecture.  Matrix operations;  error analysis;  norms of vectors and matrices;  transformations;  matrix functions; numerical solutions of systems of linear equations;  stability;  matrix inversion;  eigen value problems; approximations

title: Numerical Analysis II
description: (Prerequisites: CSE 1233 or equivalent. MA 3113 and MA 3253 ).  Three hours lecture.  Numerical solution of equations; error analysis; finite difference methods; numerical differentiation and integration; series expansions; difference equations; numerical solution of differential equations

title: Statistics in Finance
description: (Pre-requisite: MA 2733 , ST/ MA 3123 ). Three hours lecture. This course aims to give an account of the main uses of probability and statistics in finance. It will cover mathematical and statistical aspects of interest and insurance, mean (expected return) and variance aspects of portfolios with multiple assets, efficient frontier and optimal portfolios. (Same as ST 4333 /6333)

title: Mathematical Modeling with Biological and Ecological Applications
description: (Prerequisites: MA 2743 & MA 3253 ). Three hours lecture. This course covers the mathematical theory of classical and modern mathematical models relating to biological and ecological applications

title: Introduction to Partial Differential Equations
description: (Prerequisite: MA 3253 ). Three hours lecture. Linear operators: linear first order equations; the wave equation; Green's function and Sturm-Liouville problems; Fourier series; the heat equation; Laplace's equation

title: Introduction to Probability
description: (Prerequisite: MA 2733 ). Three hours lecture. Basic concepts of probability, conditional probability, independence, random variables, discrete and continuous probability distributions, moment generating function, moments, special distributions, central limit theorem. (Same as ST 4523 /6523)

title: Introduction to Probability and Random Processes
description: (Prerequisites: MA 3113 and MA 2743 ). Three hours lecture. Probability, law of large numbers, central limit theorem, sampling distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, linear regression, random processes, correlation functions, frequency and time domain analysis. (Credit can not be earned for this course and MA/ ST 4523 /6523)

title: Introduction to Mathematical Statistics I
description: (Prerequisite: MA 2743 .) Three hours lecture. Combinatorics; probability, random variables, discrete and continuous distributions, generating functions, moments, special distributions, multivariate distributions, independence, distributions of functions of random variables. (Same as ST 4543 /6543.)

title: Introduction to Mathematical Statistics II
description: (Prerequisite: MA 4543 /6543.) Three hours lecture. Continuation of MA- ST 4543 /6543. Transformations, sampling distributions, limiting distributions, point estimation, interval estimation, hypothesis testing, likelihood ratio tests, analysis of variance, regression, chi-square tests. (Same as ST 4573 /6573.)

title: Advanced Calculus I
description: (Prerequisite: MA 2743 and MA 3053 ). Three hours lecture. Theoretical investigation of functions; limits; differentiability and related topics in calculus

title: Advanced Calculus II
description: (Prerequisite: MA 4633 /6633). Three hours lecture. Rigorous development of the definite integral; sequences and series of functions; convergence criteria; improper integrals

title: Linear Programming
description: (Prerequisites: MA 3113 ).Three hours lecture. Theory and application of linear programming; simplex algorithm,revised simplex algorithm, duality and sensitivity analysis, transportation and assignment problem algorithms,interger and goal programming. (Same as IE 4733 /6733)

title: Applied Complex Variables
description: (Prerequisite: MA 2743 ). Three hours lecture. Analytic functions: Taylor and Laurent expansions; Cauchy theorems and integrals; residues; contour integration; introduction to conformal mapping

title: Mathematical Analysis I
description: (Prerequisite: MA 4633 /6633 or equivalent). Three hours lecture. Metric and topological spaces; functions of bounded variation and differentiability in normed spaces

title: Mathematical Analysis II
description: (Prerequisite: MA 4933 /6933). Three hours lecture. Riemann-Stieltjes integration, sequences and series of functions; implicit function theorem; multiple integration

title: Elementary Topology
description: (Prerequisite: MA 4633 /6633). Three hours lecture. Definition of a topological space, metric space, continuity in metric spaces and topological spaces; sequences; accumulation points; compactness, separability

Nonparametric and distribution-free methods, including inferences for proportions, contingency table analysis, goodness of fit tests, statistical methods based on rank order, and measures of association

description: (Prerequisite: any introductory statistics course) Three hours lecture. This course investigates technical aspects of gambling and gaming. The theoretical underpinnings of all games of chance lie in probability theory. The rules of several games of chance will be examined, then statistical quantification of risk and reward are developed

description: (Prerequisite: MA/ ST 4523 or MA/ ST 4543 /6543) Three hours lecture. Introductory treatment of probability models and stochastic processes such as finite-state Markov chains, Poisson Processes, dynamic programming, hazard analysis, decision analysis, and simulation. This course considers both theory and application

title: First Year Seminar
description: One hour lecture. First-year seminars explore a diverse array of topics that provide students with an opportunity to learn about a specific discipline from skilled faculty members

title: Introduction to Philosophy
description: Three hours lecture. An introduction to the major ideals and methods of philosophy. At least one philosophic classic is read, usually one suitable for orientation purposes. Honors section available

title: Introduction to Logic
description: Three hours lecture. A development of practical ability in the major forms of valid argumentation concluding with a consideration of the universal and existential operators

title: Introduction to Ethics
description: Three hours lecture. A study of the specific considerations, such as facts, feelings, principles, values and conflicts, which influence the making of concrete moral decisions

title: Special Topics in Philosophy and Religion:  1-9 hours.
